members.name: relu
members.signature: relu(x: Tensor, inplace: bool = False)
members.summary: Apply the rectified linear unit function element-wise.
members.extended:
The simplest and most widely-used activation function in modern deep
learning.  Zeros out negative inputs and passes positive inputs
through unchanged — a piecewise-linear sparsifier that gives neural
networks their nonlinearity without saturating gradients for large
positive activations.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description:
Reserved for API compatibility — currently a no-op (Lucid always
allocates a fresh output).

returns.description: Element-wise ReLU output, same shape and dtype as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import relu
>>> relu(lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0]))
Tensor([0., 0., 0., 1., 2.])
notes:
Mathematical definition:



$$
\text{ReLU}(x) = \max(0,\, x)
$$

Gradient is the indicator $\partial \text{ReLU}/\partial x = \mathbb{1}_{x > 0}$ — zero for negative inputs, one for positive,
undefined at exactly zero (convention: 0).  This causes the **dying
ReLU** problem: a neuron that always produces negative pre-activations
receives no gradient and stops learning.  Variants like
`leaky_relu`, `elu`, `gelu`, and `silu`
address this by allowing a small (or smooth) gradient for negative
inputs.

Despite its simplicity, ReLU remains a strong default for CNN
backbones; transformers commonly prefer `gelu` for its smooth
non-monotonic shape.

members.name: leaky_relu
members.signature: leaky_relu(x: Tensor, negative_slope: float = 0.01, inplace: bool = False)
members.summary: Leaky rectified linear unit activation.
members.extended:
A simple modification of `relu` that lets a small, non-zero gradient
pass through for negative inputs.  This avoids the *dying ReLU* problem
where neurons stuck on the negative side stop updating because their
gradient is exactly zero.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape; activation is applied element-wise.

parameters.name: negative_slope
parameters.description:
Slope $\alpha$ of the linear branch for negative inputs.
Default `0.01`.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Accepted for API compatibility; currently ignored.

returns.description: Activated tensor with the same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import leaky_relu
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0])
>>> leaky_relu(x, negative_slope=0.1)
Tensor([-0.2000, -0.1000,  0.0000,  1.0000])
notes:
$$
\text{LeakyReLU}(x) = \max(0, x) + \alpha \min(0, x)
$$

The derivative is $1$ for $x > 0$ and $\alpha$ for
$x \le 0$.  Unlike `relu`, the derivative is everywhere
non-zero (assuming $\alpha \ne 0$), so gradient signal continues
to flow through inactive units.  Use Leaky ReLU when ReLU networks are
showing many permanently dead neurons; for a learnable slope, see
`prelu`.

members.name: elu
members.signature: elu(x: Tensor, alpha: float = 1.0, inplace: bool = False)
members.summary: Exponential linear unit activation.
members.extended:
A smooth alternative to `relu` that saturates to $-\alpha$ on
the negative side instead of clipping at zero.  The exponential branch
pushes the mean activation closer to zero, which speeds up training by
reducing internal covariate shift (Clevert et al. 2015).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape; activation is element-wise.

parameters.name: alpha
parameters.description:
Saturation value $\alpha > 0$ for negative inputs.
Default `1.0`.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Accepted for API compatibility; currently ignored.

returns.description: Activated tensor with the same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import elu
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0])
>>> elu(x)
Tensor([-0.8647, -0.6321,  0.0000,  1.0000])
notes:
$$
\text{ELU}(x) = \begin{cases}
    x & x > 0 \\
    \alpha (e^x - 1) & x \le 0
\end{cases}
$$

Continuous and once-differentiable at the origin (derivative is
$1$ from above and $\alpha$ from below — equal when
$\alpha = 1$).  Unlike ReLU it produces negative outputs, which
helps push activation means toward zero; unlike Leaky ReLU it bounds
the negative tail, providing implicit noise robustness.

members.name: celu
members.signature: celu(x: Tensor, alpha: float = 1.0, inplace: bool = False)
members.summary: Continuously-differentiable Exponential Linear Unit (Barron 2017).
members.extended:
A reparameterisation of `elu` whose first derivative is
continuous at the origin for *every* $\alpha > 0$, not only the
specific value $\alpha = 1$.  This makes CELU friendlier to
optimisers that exploit smooth gradients.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape; activation is element-wise.

parameters.name: alpha
parameters.description:
Saturation value $\alpha > 0$ for negative inputs.
Default `1.0`.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Accepted for API compatibility; currently ignored.

returns.description: Activated tensor with the same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import celu
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0])
>>> celu(x, alpha=1.0)
Tensor([-0.8647, -0.6321,  0.0000,  1.0000])
notes:
$$
\text{CELU}(x) = \max(0, x) + \min\!\big(0, \alpha (e^{x/\alpha} - 1)\big)
$$

At the origin both branches have derivative $1$ regardless of
$\alpha$, removing the kink ELU has when $\alpha \ne 1$.
Reduces to ELU when $\alpha = 1$.

members.name: selu
members.signature: selu(x: Tensor, inplace: bool = False)
members.summary: Scaled exponential linear unit activation.
members.extended:
Self-normalising activation from [Klambauer et al. (2017)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.02515): when used in
a fully-connected network with LeCun-normal initialised weights, the
fixed scale $\lambda$ and slope $\alpha$ drive the
activations toward zero-mean, unit-variance fixed points without the
need for explicit batch / layer normalisation.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape; activation is element-wise.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Accepted for API compatibility; currently ignored.

returns.description: Activated tensor with the same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import selu
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-1.0, 0.0, 1.0])
>>> selu(x)
Tensor([-1.1113,  0.0000,  1.0507])
notes:
$$
\text{SELU}(x) = \lambda \, \text{ELU}(x, \alpha) =
\lambda \begin{cases}
    x & x > 0 \\
    \alpha (e^x - 1) & x \le 0
\end{cases}
$$

with the (non-tunable) constants



$$
\lambda \approx 1.0507, \qquad \alpha \approx 1.6733,
$$

chosen so that the activation has a zero-mean unit-variance attractor
fixed point.  Pair with `alpha_dropout` and LeCun-normal weight init
to retain the self-normalising property.

members.name: gelu
members.signature: gelu(x: Tensor, approximate: str = 'none')
members.summary: Gaussian Error Linear Unit activation.
members.extended:
Smooth, non-monotonic activation that has largely replaced ReLU in
transformer architectures.  Approximates $x \cdot \mathbb{1}_{x>0}$
but is differentiable everywhere and lets a small negative signal pass
through, which improves gradient flow in deep networks.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape; activation is element-wise.

parameters.name: approximate
parameters.description:
Either `"none"` (default, exact formula via `erf`) or
`"tanh"` (faster polynomial approximation used in BERT /
Hendrycks 2016).

returns.description: Activated tensor with the same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import gelu
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> gelu(x)
Tensor([-0.1587,  0.0000,  0.8413,  1.9545])
notes:
Exact form:



$$
\text{GELU}(x) = x \, \Phi(x)
= \frac{x}{2}\left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]
$$

where $\Phi$ is the standard normal CDF.  The `"tanh"`
approximation is



$$
\text{GELU}(x) \approx \frac{x}{2}\left[1 +
\tanh\!\left(\sqrt{\tfrac{2}{\pi}}\,(x + 0.044715\, x^3)\right)\right].
$$

Unlike ReLU, GELU has a non-zero gradient everywhere — useful for
training very deep transformer stacks.  Introduced by
[Hendrycks & Gimpel (2016)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1606.08415) and adopted widely after BERT.

members.name: silu
members.signature: silu(x: Tensor, inplace: bool = False)
members.summary: Sigmoid Linear Unit (a.k.a. Swish-1) activation.
members.extended:
Smooth, non-monotonic activation popularised by Ramachandran et al.
(2017) as the result of a neural architecture search over activation
functions.  Equivalent to Swish with $\beta = 1$.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape; activation is element-wise.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Accepted for API compatibility; currently ignored.

returns.description: Activated tensor with the same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import silu
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> silu(x)
Tensor([-0.2384, -0.2689,  0.0000,  0.7311,  1.7616])
notes:
$$
\text{SiLU}(x) = x \, \sigma(x) = \frac{x}{1 + e^{-x}}
$$

Has a small negative bump near $x \approx -1.28$ (minimum value
$\approx -0.278$); approaches 0 from below for large negative
input and approaches the identity for large positive input.  The
derivative is



$$
\text{SiLU}'(x) = \sigma(x) + x\sigma(x)(1 - \sigma(x)),
$$

which is smooth and unbounded above.  Often outperforms ReLU in deep
CNNs and is the default in EfficientNet and many MoE blocks.

members.name: mish
members.signature: mish(x: Tensor)
members.summary: Mish activation function.
members.extended:
Self-regularising non-monotonic activation introduced by Misra (2019).
Like `silu` it has a small negative dip and approaches the
identity for large positive inputs, but its tanh-of-softplus form gives
slightly smoother gradients which tend to help convergence in CV tasks
(YOLOv4 uses it throughout).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape; activation is element-wise.

returns.description: Activated tensor with the same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import mish
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> mish(x)
Tensor([-0.2525, -0.3034,  0.0000,  0.8651,  1.9440])
notes:
$$
\text{Mish}(x) = x \, \tanh\!\big(\text{softplus}(x)\big)
              = x \, \tanh\!\big(\ln(1 + e^x)\big)
$$

$C^\infty$-smooth, non-monotonic, unbounded above and bounded
below (minimum $\approx -0.3088$).  Empirically outperforms
ReLU/Swish on some object-detection benchmarks at modest extra cost.

members.name: hardswish
members.signature: hardswish(x: Tensor)
members.summary: Hard Swish activation — piecewise linear approximation of `silu`.
members.extended:
Introduced in MobileNetV3 (Howard et al. 2019) as a compute-friendly
replacement for the sigmoid in Swish: the exponential is approximated
by a clipped linear function, which is far cheaper on mobile / quantised
hardware while preserving the shape of the activation.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape; activation is element-wise.

returns.description: Activated tensor with the same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import hardswish
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-4.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 4.0])
>>> hardswish(x)
Tensor([ 0.0000, -0.3333,  0.0000,  0.6667,  4.0000])
notes:
$$
\text{HardSwish}(x) = x \cdot \frac{\text{ReLU6}(x + 3)}{6}
= \begin{cases}
    0 & x \le -3 \\
    x(x+3)/6 & -3 < x < 3 \\
    x & x \ge 3
\end{cases}
$$

Continuous but only piecewise-$C^1$ (the second derivative jumps
at $x = \pm 3$).  Cheap to evaluate and to quantise — preferred
over `silu` in mobile architectures.

members.name: hardsigmoid
members.signature: hardsigmoid(x: Tensor)
members.summary: Hard sigmoid — piecewise linear approximation of `sigmoid`.
members.extended:
Avoids the cost of an exponential by clipping a scaled-and-shifted
identity to $[0, 1]$.  Widely used as the gating function inside
quantised mobile networks (MobileNetV3 Squeeze-and-Excitation blocks).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape; activation is element-wise.

returns.description:
Activated tensor with the same shape as `x`, values in
$[0, 1]$.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import hardsigmoid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-4.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 4.0])
>>> hardsigmoid(x)
Tensor([0.0000, 0.3333, 0.5000, 0.6667, 1.0000])
notes:
$$
\text{HardSigmoid}(x) = \begin{cases}
    0 & x \le -3 \\
    x/6 + 1/2 & -3 < x < 3 \\
    1 & x \ge 3
\end{cases}
$$

Matches `sigmoid` exactly at $x = 0$ (both return `0.5`)
but is only piecewise-linear.  Derivative is $1/6$ in the linear
region and $0$ in the saturated regions.

members.name: sigmoid
members.signature: sigmoid(x: Tensor)
members.summary: Logistic sigmoid activation.
members.extended:
Squashes any real-valued input into the open interval $(0, 1)$.
Historically the canonical activation for binary classification heads
and the gating function in LSTM / GRU cells.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape; activation is element-wise.

returns.description:
Activated tensor with the same shape as `x`, values in
$(0, 1)$.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import sigmoid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, 0.0, 2.0])
>>> sigmoid(x)
Tensor([0.1192, 0.5000, 0.8808])
notes:
$$
\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}
$$

Derivative $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)(1 - \sigma(x)) \le 0.25$.
The bounded derivative is the classical cause of the vanishing
gradient problem in deep feed-forward stacks, so for hidden layers
prefer ReLU / GELU / SiLU.  For binary classification logits, use
`logsigmoid` to compute $\log\sigma(x)$ in a numerically
stable way.

members.name: tanh
members.signature: tanh(x: Tensor)
members.summary: Hyperbolic tangent activation.
members.extended:
A rescaled, recentred sigmoid that maps the real line to
$(-1, 1)$.  Because outputs are zero-centred, tanh is generally
preferred over `sigmoid` for hidden layers in shallow networks
and is the canonical squashing function in RNN cells.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape; activation is element-wise.

returns.description:
Activated tensor with the same shape as `x`, values in
$(-1, 1)$.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import tanh
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, 0.0, 2.0])
>>> tanh(x)
Tensor([-0.9640,  0.0000,  0.9640])
notes:
$$
\tanh(x) = \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}}
         = 2\sigma(2x) - 1
$$

Derivative $1 - \tanh^2(x)$, maximum value $1$ at the
origin.  Saturates for $|x| \gtrsim 3$; in deep networks this
leads to vanishing gradients, so modern feed-forward stacks prefer
ReLU-family activations.

members.name: softmax
members.signature: softmax(x: Tensor, dim: int | None = None)
members.summary: Apply the softmax function along a dimension.
members.extended:
Converts a vector of real-valued logits into a probability
distribution: the outputs are non-negative and sum to one along
`dim`.  The central tool for multi-class classification heads and
attention weights.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape (the "logits").

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description:
Dimension along which softmax is computed.  Defaults to `-1`
(the last axis).

returns.description:
Same-shape tensor whose entries along `dim` form a probability
simplex (each non-negative, summing to 1).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import softmax
>>> logits = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 2.0, 3.0]])
>>> p = softmax(logits, dim=1)
>>> p
Tensor([[0.0900, 0.2447, 0.6652]])
>>> p.sum(dim=1)
Tensor([1.0000])
notes:
Mathematical definition (per-vector along `dim`):



$$
\text{softmax}(x)_i = \frac{e^{x_i}}{\sum_j e^{x_j}}
$$

A naïve implementation overflows for large positive `x`; the engine
uses the standard log-sum-exp shift $x_i \mapsto x_i - \max_j x_j$
to evaluate it in finite precision.

For loss computation, prefer `log_softmax` followed by
`nll_loss` (or `cross_entropy` end-to-end), since the
composition of `log(softmax(...))` loses precision.  The gradient
has the convenient closed form
$\partial p_i / \partial x_j = p_i (\delta_{ij} - p_j)$ —
the Jacobian factorises out cleanly during backprop through softmax-CE.

members.name: log_softmax
members.signature: log_softmax(x: Tensor, dim: int | None = None)
members.summary: Numerically stable log-softmax along a dimension.
members.extended:
Equivalent to `log(softmax(x, dim))` but computed in a way that
avoids overflow in the exponentials and the loss of precision incurred
by taking the logarithm of small softmax probabilities.  Almost always
the right thing to use as the input to negative-log-likelihood / NLL
classification losses.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input logits of any shape.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description:
Dimension along which the log-softmax is computed.  Defaults to
the last dimension (`-1`).

returns.description:
Log-probabilities of the same shape as `x`, summing-to-one on
the exponentiated scale along `dim`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import log_softmax
>>> logits = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 2.0, 3.0]])
>>> log_softmax(logits, dim=1)
Tensor([[-2.4076, -1.4076, -0.4076]])
notes:
$$
\text{log\_softmax}(x)_i = x_i - \log\!\sum_j e^{x_j}
= (x_i - m) - \log\!\sum_j e^{x_j - m}, \quad m = \max_j x_j
$$

The $-m$ shift inside the exponent is the key numerical trick:
it makes every exponent non-positive so $e^{x_j - m} \in (0, 1]$
and the sum is bounded.  Pairing `log_softmax` with
`nll_loss` is numerically equivalent to — and more stable than —
`softmax` followed by `cross_entropy`.

members.name: softplus
members.signature: softplus(x: Tensor, beta: float = 1.0, threshold: float = 20.0)
members.summary: Smooth approximation of `relu`.
members.extended:
Softplus is a strictly positive, $C^\infty$ smooth function that
behaves like $x$ for large positive inputs and like $e^x$
for large negative inputs.  Its derivative is `sigmoid`, which is
why softplus appears naturally as the log-partition of a Bernoulli and
as the analytic primitive of the logistic function.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape; activation is element-wise.

parameters.name: beta
parameters.description:
Sharpness parameter $\beta > 0$.  Larger `beta` makes the
curve approach a hard ReLU.  Default `1.0`.

parameters.name: threshold
parameters.description:
For numerical stability the formula collapses to the identity
$x$ whenever $\beta x > \text{threshold}$ (the
$e^{\beta x}$ term would otherwise overflow).  Default
`20.0`.

returns.description: Activated tensor with the same shape as `x`, strictly positive.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import softplus
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, 0.0, 2.0])
>>> softplus(x)
Tensor([0.1269, 0.6931, 2.1269])
notes:
$$
\text{softplus}_\beta(x) = \frac{1}{\beta}\log\!\big(1 + e^{\beta x}\big)
$$

Derivative is $\sigma(\beta x)$.  The overflow guard at
`threshold` exploits that for large $\beta x$ the function is
indistinguishable from $x$ in finite precision.  The engine's
bare `softplus` kernel implements the special case
$\beta = 1$, $\text{threshold} \to \infty$; when either
argument deviates the full formula is composed so autograd still
flows through the existing backward nodes.

members.name: relu6
members.signature: relu6(x: Tensor, inplace: bool = False)
members.summary: Rectified linear unit clipped at six.
members.extended:
Introduced for quantisation-friendly mobile architectures
(MobileNetV1 / V2): bounding ReLU above keeps activations inside a
small fixed range so that low-precision integer arithmetic does not
overflow.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape; activation is element-wise.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Accepted for API compatibility; currently ignored.

returns.description:
Activated tensor with the same shape as `x`, values in
$[0, 6]$.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import relu6
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-1.0, 0.0, 3.0, 7.0])
>>> relu6(x)
Tensor([0.0000, 0.0000, 3.0000, 6.0000])
notes:
$$
\text{ReLU6}(x) = \min(\max(0, x), 6)
$$

Derivative is $1$ on $(0, 6)$ and $0$ outside —
so beyond suffering from "dead neurons" on the negative side, ReLU6
also kills gradients above $6$.  The trade-off is worthwhile
for int8 quantisation where the symmetric $[0, 6]$ range maps
cleanly to a small dynamic-range integer codebook.

members.name: softmin
members.signature: softmin(x: Tensor, dim: int | None = None)
members.summary: Softmin — softmax applied to the negation of the input.
members.extended:
Useful when small input values should receive high probability, e.g.
when `x` represents distances or costs and a soft-argmin is needed.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description:
Dimension along which softmin is computed.  Defaults to the last
dimension (`-1`).

returns.description:
Tensor of the same shape with values summing to `1` along
`dim`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import softmin
>>> x = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 2.0, 3.0]])
>>> softmin(x, dim=1)
Tensor([[0.6652, 0.2447, 0.0900]])
notes:
$$
\text{softmin}(x)_i = \text{softmax}(-x)_i = \frac{e^{-x_i}}{\sum_j e^{-x_j}}
$$

Exactly equivalent to `softmax` on the negated input; produced
as a separate function purely for readability when modelling
minimum-cost attention or temperature-scaled retrieval.

members.name: glu
members.signature: glu(x: Tensor, dim: int = -1)
members.summary: Gated Linear Unit (Dauphin et al. 2017).
members.extended:
Splits the input in half along `dim` and multiplies the first half
element-wise by the sigmoid of the second.  Acts as a learnable gate
that lets the network modulate information flow per channel; widely
used in sequence models and Conformer-style speech architectures.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description:
Input tensor.  The size along `dim` must be even — it is split
into two halves $a$ and $b$ of equal size.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Dimension along which to split.  Default `-1`.

returns.description:
Output tensor whose shape matches `x` except along `dim`,
where the size is halved.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import glu
>>> x = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 2.0, 0.0, 1.0]])  # split → a=[1,2], b=[0,1]
>>> glu(x, dim=-1)
Tensor([[0.5000, 1.4621]])
notes:
$$
\text{GLU}(x) = a \odot \sigma(b),
\qquad x = [a; b] \text{ split along } \text{dim}
$$

The sigmoid gate $\sigma(b)$ selects how much of $a$ to
pass through.  Because the gate is multiplicative the derivative is
well-behaved (no saturation in $a$'s direction), which helps
train deeper stacks than a plain feed-forward layer would allow.

members.name: prelu
members.signature: prelu(x: Tensor, weight: Tensor)
members.summary: Parametric Rectified Linear Unit (He et al. 2015).
members.extended:
Variant of `leaky_relu` whose negative slope is a **learnable**
parameter rather than a fixed hyperparameter.  In practice `weight`
may be a scalar (shared across channels) or a per-channel vector,
matching the convention of the reference framework's `nn.PReLU`.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description:
Learnable slope $\alpha$.  Either a scalar tensor (slope
shared across all elements) or a 1-D tensor broadcastable against
`x` along the channel dimension.

returns.description: Activated tensor with the same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import prelu
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0])
>>> w = lucid.tensor(0.25)
>>> prelu(x, w)
Tensor([-0.5000, -0.2500,  0.0000,  1.0000])
notes:
$$
\text{PReLU}(x) = \max(0, x) + \alpha \min(0, x)
$$

Allows the optimiser to discover the *optimal* negative-side slope
per channel; the original paper showed this reaches super-human
ImageNet accuracy.  When $\alpha = 0$ recovers `relu`; for
a fixed non-zero $\alpha$ see `leaky_relu`.

members.name: hardshrink
members.signature: hardshrink(x: Tensor, lambd: float = 0.5)
members.summary: Hard shrinkage operator.
members.extended:
Sets elements with magnitude below $\lambda$ to zero, leaves
larger-magnitude elements untouched.  Standard tool for sparse coding
and the proximal operator of the $\ell_0$ "pseudonorm" on a
coefficient-by-coefficient basis.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor.

parameters.name: lambd
parameters.description: Threshold $\lambda \ge 0$.  Default `0.5`.

returns.description: Shrunk tensor with the same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import hardshrink
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-1.0, -0.3, 0.0, 0.3, 1.0])
>>> hardshrink(x, lambd=0.5)
Tensor([-1.0000,  0.0000,  0.0000,  0.0000,  1.0000])

members.name: tanhshrink
members.signature: tanhshrink(x: Tensor)
members.summary: Tanh shrinkage activation.
members.extended:
Behaves like a smooth high-pass filter: subtracts the tanh-saturated
component of the input, leaving small linear residuals near zero and
near-linear behaviour for large magnitudes.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape; activation is element-wise.

returns.description: Activated tensor with the same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import tanhshrink
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -0.5, 0.0, 0.5, 2.0])
>>> tanhshrink(x)
Tensor([-1.0360, -0.0379,  0.0000,  0.0379,  1.0360])
notes:
$$
\text{TanhShrink}(x) = x - \tanh(x)
$$

For $|x| \ll 1$, $\tanh(x) \approx x - x^3/3$, so
$\text{TanhShrink}(x) \approx x^3/3$ — small signals are
cubically suppressed.  For $|x| \gg 1$, $\tanh$ saturates
to $\pm 1$ so the output behaves like $x \mp 1$.
$C^\infty$-smooth and monotonic.

members.name: softshrink
members.signature: softshrink(x: Tensor, lambd: float = 0.5)
members.summary: Soft shrinkage operator — proximal operator of $\lambda \|\cdot\|_1$.
members.extended:
Translates the input toward zero by $\lambda$ and zeros out
everything in the dead zone $[-\lambda, \lambda]$.  Used in
iterative-shrinkage solvers (ISTA / FISTA) and in sparse autoencoder
decoders.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor.

parameters.name: lambd
parameters.description: Shrinkage threshold $\lambda \ge 0$.  Default `0.5`.

returns.description: Shrunk tensor with the same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import softshrink
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-1.0, -0.3, 0.0, 0.3, 1.0])
>>> softshrink(x, lambd=0.5)
Tensor([-0.5000,  0.0000,  0.0000,  0.0000,  0.5000])
notes:
$$
\text{SoftShrink}(x) = \begin{cases}
    x - \lambda & x > \lambda \\
    x + \lambda & x < -\lambda \\
    0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Continuous everywhere (unlike `hardshrink`), with derivative
$1$ outside the dead zone and $0$ inside it.  Coincides
with the closed-form solution of
$\arg\min_y \tfrac12 (y - x)^2 + \lambda |y|$.

members.name: normalize
members.signature: normalize(x: Tensor, p: float = 2.0, dim: int = 1, eps: float = 1e-12)
members.summary: Normalize a tensor to unit $L_p$ norm along a dimension.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Exponent of the norm (default: 2.0 for Euclidean norm).

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Dimension along which to normalize (default: 1).

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Small value added to the denominator for numerical stability.

returns.description: Normalized tensor with unit $L_p$ norm along `dim`.

examples:
>>> x = lucid.tensor([[3.0, 4.0]])
>>> F.normalize(x, p=2, dim=1)
tensor([[0.6, 0.8]])
notes:
$$
\hat{x}_i = \frac{x_i}{\max\!\big(\|x\|_p,\, \epsilon\big)},
\qquad \|x\|_p = \Big(\sum_i |x_i|^p\Big)^{1/p}
$$

along the chosen dimension.  The `eps` clamp avoids division by
zero when the slice is all zeros.  Use `p=2` for cosine-similarity
style embeddings, `p=1` for probability-simplex projection-like
behaviour, and `p=inf` for max-norm clipping.

members.name: cosine_similarity
members.signature: cosine_similarity(x1: Tensor, x2: Tensor, dim: int = 1, eps: float = 1e-08)
members.summary: Cosine similarity between two tensors along a dimension.
members.extended:
Measures the angle between two vectors irrespective of their
magnitudes — the natural similarity metric for unit-norm embedding
spaces (contrastive learning, retrieval, face verification).

parameters.name: x1
parameters.description: Tensors of the same shape.

parameters.name: x2
parameters.description: Tensors of the same shape.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description:
Dimension along which the dot product / norms are taken.
Default `1`.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description:
Lower bound on the denominator to avoid division by zero.
Default `1e-8`.

returns.description:
Similarity tensor whose shape matches the inputs with `dim`
reduced, values in $[-1, 1]$.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import cosine_similarity
>>> a = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 0.0]])
>>> b = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 1.0]])
>>> cosine_similarity(a, b, dim=1)
Tensor([0.7071])
notes:
$$
\text{cosine\_sim}(x_1, x_2) =
\frac{x_1 \cdot x_2}{\max(\|x_1\|_2 \, \|x_2\|_2,\, \epsilon)}
$$

Internally each input is $L_2$-normalised along `dim` via
`normalize`, after which the dot product is exactly the cosine.
For pairwise similarity matrices, use `unsqueeze` to broadcast the
inputs into rank-3 tensors before calling this function.

members.name: pairwise_distance
members.signature: pairwise_distance(x1: Tensor, x2: Tensor, p: float = 2.0, eps: float = 1e-06, keepdim: bool = False)
members.summary: Element-wise $L_p$ distance between two equally-shaped tensors.
members.extended:
Computes the per-row $L_p$ norm of the difference — the standard
metric in metric-learning triplet and contrastive losses.

parameters.name: x1
parameters.description:
Tensors of the same shape.  Distance is reduced over the **last**
dimension.

parameters.name: x2
parameters.description:
Tensors of the same shape.  Distance is reduced over the **last**
dimension.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description:
Order of the norm.  `2` for Euclidean, `1` for Manhattan.
Default `2.0`.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description:
Small constant added to `|x1 - x2|` before the power so the
derivative is finite even at coincident points.  Default `1e-6`.

parameters.name: keepdim
parameters.description:
If `True`, retain the reduced dimension with size `1`.
Default `False`.

returns.description: Distance tensor.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import pairwise_distance
>>> a = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 2.0]])
>>> b = lucid.tensor([[4.0, 6.0]])
>>> pairwise_distance(a, b, p=2.0)
Tensor([5.0000])
notes:
$$
d_p(x_1, x_2) = \big\| |x_1 - x_2| + \epsilon \big\|_p
= \Big(\sum_i (|x_{1,i} - x_{2,i}| + \epsilon)^p\Big)^{1/p}
$$

The `eps` shift inside the norm matters for gradient stability — at
$x_1 = x_2$ the bare $L_p$ distance is non-differentiable
when $p < 2$; the offset converts the kink into a finite-slope
smooth point.

members.name: hardtanh
members.signature: hardtanh(x: Tensor, min_val: float = -1.0, max_val: float = 1.0, inplace: bool = False)
members.summary: Hardtanh — element-wise clamp to $[\text{min\_val}, \text{max\_val}]$.
members.extended:
A cheap piecewise-linear surrogate for `tanh` whose forward and
backward passes require no transcendental functions.  Standard
activation in compact / quantised RNN cells.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape.

parameters.name: min_val
parameters.description: Lower clamp.  Default `-1.0`.

parameters.name: max_val
parameters.description: Upper clamp.  Default `1.0`.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Accepted for API compatibility; currently ignored.

returns.description: Clamped tensor with the same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import hardtanh
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -0.5, 0.0, 0.5, 2.0])
>>> hardtanh(x)
Tensor([-1.0000, -0.5000,  0.0000,  0.5000,  1.0000])
notes:
$$
\text{HardTanh}(x) = \begin{cases}
    \text{max\_val} & x > \text{max\_val} \\
    x & \text{min\_val} \le x \le \text{max\_val} \\
    \text{min\_val} & x < \text{min\_val}
\end{cases}
$$

Derivative is $1$ inside the linear region and $0$ in the
saturated regions — gradients stop flowing through saturated units, a
drawback compared to smooth tanh but acceptable when the linear region
covers the bulk of activations.

members.name: logsigmoid
members.signature: logsigmoid(x: Tensor)
members.summary: Numerically stable $\log \sigma(x)$.
members.extended:
Direct evaluation of $\log(1 / (1 + e^{-x}))$ underflows once
$\sigma(x)$ is below machine epsilon (around $x \lesssim -37$
for float32).  Rewriting as $-\operatorname{softplus}(-x)$
matches the same value while staying finite for arbitrarily negative
inputs.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input logits.

returns.description:
$\log \sigma(x)$ with the same shape as `x`, values in
$(-\infty, 0]$.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import logsigmoid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-10.0, 0.0, 10.0])
>>> logsigmoid(x)
Tensor([-10.0000,  -0.6931,  -0.0000])
notes:
$$
\log\sigma(x) = -\log(1 + e^{-x}) = -\operatorname{softplus}(-x)
$$

Frequently the building block of binary cross-entropy with logits
(BCE-with-logits) and of importance-weighting computations in RL.

members.name: softsign
members.signature: softsign(x: Tensor)
members.summary: Softsign activation.
members.extended:
A bounded $(-1, 1)$ activation that saturates **polynomially**
rather than exponentially — its tails are much heavier than
`tanh`'s, so gradients vanish more slowly in deep networks.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape; activation is element-wise.

returns.description:
Activated tensor with the same shape as `x`, values in
$(-1, 1)$.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import softsign
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> softsign(x)
Tensor([-0.6667, -0.5000,  0.0000,  0.5000,  0.6667])
notes:
$$
\text{softsign}(x) = \frac{x}{1 + |x|}, \qquad
\text{softsign}'(x) = \frac{1}{(1 + |x|)^2}
$$

Derivative decays like $1/x^2$ rather than $\tanh$'s
$1/\cosh^2$, so saturation is gentler.  Lacks the bi-Lipschitz
smoothness of tanh near zero (kink at $x = 0$ from the absolute
value) but is monotone and zero-centred.

members.name: threshold
members.signature: threshold(x: Tensor, threshold: float, value: float, inplace: bool = False)
members.summary: Threshold activation — gate elements by a scalar cutoff.
members.extended:
Replaces every element of `x` that is **not** strictly greater than
`threshold` with the constant `value`.  A generalisation of
`relu` (recovered by `threshold=0, value=0`) and a common
component of dead-zone non-linearities.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor.

parameters.name: threshold
parameters.description: Cutoff $t$.  Elements satisfying $x > t$ are kept.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Replacement constant for elements not above the threshold.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Accepted for API compatibility; currently ignored.

returns.description: Thresholded tensor with the same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import threshold
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-1.0, 0.0, 0.5, 1.0])
>>> threshold(x, threshold=0.5, value=-99.0)
Tensor([-99.0000, -99.0000, -99.0000,   1.0000])

members.name: rrelu
members.signature: rrelu(x: Tensor, lower: float = 1.0 / 8.0, upper: float = 1.0 / 3.0, training: bool = False, inplace: bool = False)
members.summary: Randomised leaky ReLU (Xu et al. 2015).
members.extended:
A regularised variant of `leaky_relu`: during **training** every
negative element is scaled by an i.i.d. uniform slope, injecting
activation noise on the negative side.  At **evaluation** time the
expectation of that uniform draw is used, so the function becomes
deterministic.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor.

parameters.name: lower
parameters.description: Lower bound of the uniform slope distribution.  Default `1/8`.

parameters.name: upper
parameters.description: Upper bound of the uniform slope distribution.  Default `1/3`.

parameters.name: training
parameters.description:
If `True` sample a fresh slope per element; if `False` use the
midpoint.  Default `False`.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Accepted for API compatibility; currently ignored.

returns.description: Activated tensor with the same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import rrelu
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-1.0, 0.0, 1.0])
>>> rrelu(x, training=False)
Tensor([-0.2292,  0.0000,  1.0000])
notes:
During training, with $a \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(\text{lower}, \text{upper})$ sampled element-wise:



$$
\text{RReLU}(x) = \begin{cases}
    x & x \ge 0 \\
    a x & x < 0
\end{cases}
$$

During evaluation, $a$ is replaced by the expectation
$(\text{lower} + \text{upper}) / 2$.  The slope randomisation
acts as a mild noise regulariser — comparable in spirit to dropout
but applied to activation slope rather than activation magnitude.

members.name: gumbel_softmax
members.signature: gumbel_softmax(logits: Tensor, tau: float = 1.0, hard: bool = False, dim: int = -1)
members.summary: Gumbel-Softmax — differentiable relaxation of categorical sampling.
members.extended:
Draws Gumbel noise, adds it to the logits, and softmaxes by
temperature $\tau$.  Lets gradients flow through what would
otherwise be a non-differentiable categorical sample — central to
differentiable discrete-action RL, VQ-VAE codebook training, and
Concrete distributions (Jang et al. / Maddison et al. 2017).

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Unnormalised log-probabilities of any shape; the last (or
`dim`-th) axis indexes the categorical alternatives.

parameters.name: tau
parameters.description:
Temperature $\tau > 0$.  Smaller $\tau$ makes the
relaxation closer to a one-hot, at the cost of larger gradient
variance.  Default `1.0`.

parameters.name: hard
parameters.description:
If `True` the forward pass returns a true one-hot vector while
the backward pass uses the soft gradient (straight-through
estimator).  Default `False`.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Dimension over which to take the softmax.  Default `-1`.

returns.description:
Sample with the same shape as `logits`.  Rows sum to `1`
along `dim`; in hard mode each row is one-hot.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import gumbel_softmax
>>> logits = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 2.0, 3.0]])
>>> y = gumbel_softmax(logits, tau=0.5, hard=True)
>>> y.sum().item()
1.0
notes:
$$
g_i \sim \text{Gumbel}(0, 1), \qquad
y_i = \frac{\exp((\log p_i + g_i)/\tau)}{\sum_j \exp((\log p_j + g_j)/\tau)}
$$

Gumbel samples are drawn as $-\log(-\log U)$ with
$U \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0,1)$, clamped away from the boundaries
to avoid $\log 0$.  In *hard* mode the result is
$y_{\text{hard}} - y_{\text{soft}}.\text{detach}() + y_{\text{soft}}$,
which evaluates to $y_{\text{hard}}$ in the forward and to
$y_{\text{soft}}$ in the backward.

members.name: relu_
members.signature: relu_(x: Tensor, inplace: bool = True)
members.summary: In-place ReLU — `x` is overwritten with `max(0, x)`.
members.extended:
Backed by the engine's native in-place ReLU kernel (no temporary
buffer).  `inplace` is accepted for API parity but ignored:
this entry point is always in-place.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input; modified in place and also returned.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description:
Ignored; retained for signature symmetry with the out-of-place
`relu`.  Default `True`.

returns.description: The same `x` tensor, now holding `max(0, x)`.

members.name: elu_
members.signature: elu_(x: Tensor, alpha: float = 1.0, inplace: bool = True)
members.summary: In-place ELU — overwrites `x` with $\text{ELU}(x, \alpha)$.
members.extended:
Computes the out-of-place result via `elu` then copies it
back into `x` (the engine has no native fused in-place kernel).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input; modified in place.

parameters.name: alpha
parameters.description: Negative-side scale parameter $\alpha$.  Default `1.0`.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Ignored; this entry point is always in-place.  Default `True`.

returns.description: The same `x` tensor, now holding `elu(x, alpha)`.

members.name: selu_
members.signature: selu_(x: Tensor, inplace: bool = True)
members.summary: In-place SELU — overwrites `x` with `selu(x)`.
members.extended:
Uses the canonical SELU coefficients (Klambauer 2017); intended
for use only inside Self-Normalising Networks where the input was
initialised with LeCun-normal and downstream blocks expect the
SELU fixed-point statistics.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input; modified in place.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Ignored.  Default `True`.

returns.description: The same `x` tensor, now holding `selu(x)`.

members.name: leaky_relu_
members.signature: leaky_relu_(x: Tensor, negative_slope: float = 0.01, inplace: bool = True)
members.summary: In-place Leaky ReLU — overwrites `x` with the leaky-rectified result.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input; modified in place.

parameters.name: negative_slope
parameters.description: Slope applied to the negative side.  Default `0.01`.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Ignored.  Default `True`.

returns.description: The same `x` tensor, now holding `leaky_relu(x, negative_slope)`.

members.name: hardtanh_
members.signature: hardtanh_(x: Tensor, min_val: float = -1.0, max_val: float = 1.0, inplace: bool = True)
members.summary: In-place hardtanh — clamps `x` to `[min_val, max_val]` in place.
members.extended:
Equivalent to `x.clamp_(min_val, max_val)` but kept under the
`nn.functional` name for activation-API symmetry.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input; modified in place.

parameters.name: min_val
parameters.description: Lower clamp bound.  Default `-1.0`.

parameters.name: max_val
parameters.description: Upper clamp bound.  Default `1.0`.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Ignored.  Default `True`.

returns.description: The same `x` tensor, now clamped.

members.name: threshold_
members.signature: threshold_(x: Tensor, threshold_val: float, value: float, inplace: bool = True)
members.summary: In-place threshold — replace entries `<= threshold_val` with `value`.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input; modified in place.

parameters.name: threshold_val
parameters.description: Cutoff; positions where `x <= threshold_val` are reset.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Replacement value for clipped positions.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Ignored.  Default `True`.

returns.description:
The same `x` tensor, with sub-threshold positions set to
`value` and the rest unchanged.

members.name: rrelu_
members.signature: rrelu_(x: Tensor, lower: float = 1.0 / 8, upper: float = 1.0 / 3, training: bool = False, inplace: bool = True)
members.summary: In-place RReLU — randomised leaky ReLU written back into `x`.
members.extended:
During training the negative-slope is sampled per-element from a
uniform $U(\text{lower}, \text{upper})$; at inference the
midpoint $(\text{lower} + \text{upper}) / 2$ is used.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input; modified in place.

parameters.name: lower
parameters.description: Lower bound of the uniform slope sampler.  Default `1/8`.

parameters.name: upper
parameters.description: Upper bound of the uniform slope sampler.  Default `1/3`.

parameters.name: training
parameters.description:
When `False` (default) uses the deterministic midpoint slope;
when `True` samples a fresh slope per element.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Ignored.  Default `True`.

returns.description: The same `x` tensor, now holding `rrelu(x, lower, upper, training)`.

members.name: linear
members.signature: linear(x: Tensor, weight: Tensor, bias: Tensor | None = None)
members.summary: Apply an affine linear transformation $y = x W^\top + b$.
members.extended:
The canonical fully-connected layer kernel — every dense / MLP /
transformer block in Lucid ultimately routes through this call.
`weight` is stored in row-major `(out_features, in_features)`
orientation (matching the rest of the framework's matmul-of-transpose
convention), and the leading batch dims of `x` are preserved
untouched.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description:
Input activations, shape `(..., in_features)`.  Any number of
leading batch dimensions is allowed.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description: Weight matrix, shape `(out_features, in_features)`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description:
Bias vector, shape `(out_features,)`.  When `None` (default)
the kernel runs with an implicit zero bias allocated on the same
device / dtype as `weight` — equivalent to the unbiased form
$y = x W^\top$.

returns.description: Output activations, shape `(..., out_features)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import linear
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 16)            # batch of 4
>>> w = lucid.randn(32, 16)
>>> b = lucid.zeros(32)
>>> linear(x, w, b).shape
(4, 32)
>>> linear(x, w).shape                # bias=None, unbiased form
(4, 32)
notes:
Implementation dispatches to the engine's `nn.linear` kernel:
a single BLAS SGEMM on the CPU stream (Accelerate) or an MLX matmul
on the GPU stream, fused with the bias addition.  Backward stores
the saved `x` / `weight` so the chain-rule path



$$
\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = W,\quad
\frac{\partial y}{\partial W} = x^\top,\quad
\frac{\partial y}{\partial b} = \mathbf{1}
$$

can be evaluated with two more SGEMMs in `LinearBackward`.
For inference-only ReLU / GELU follow-up the FusionPass picks
`fused_linear_relu` / `fused_linear_gelu` instead and
skips the activation's intermediate buffer.

members.name: bilinear
members.signature: bilinear(x1: Tensor, x2: Tensor, weight: Tensor, bias: Tensor | None = None)
members.summary: Bilinear transformation applied to two inputs.
members.extended:
Computes a quadratic interaction between every pair of features from
`x1` and `x2`.  Common in fine-grained recognition (bilinear
pooling), tensor-decomposition attention, and gated cross-modal
fusion layers.

parameters.name: x1
parameters.description: First input, shape `(..., in1_features)`.

parameters.name: x2
parameters.description: Second input, shape `(..., in2_features)`.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description: Weight tensor, shape `(out_features, in1_features, in2_features)`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description: Bias vector, shape `(out_features,)`.  Default `None`.

returns.description: Output tensor, shape `(..., out_features)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import bilinear
>>> x1 = lucid.randn(2, 3)
>>> x2 = lucid.randn(2, 4)
>>> w = lucid.randn(5, 3, 4)
>>> bilinear(x1, x2, w).shape
(2, 5)
notes:
For each output channel $k$,



$$
y_k = x_1^\top W_k x_2 + b_k,
$$

where $W_k$ is the $k$-th slice of the weight tensor.
The parameter count grows multiplicatively in
$\text{in1} \times \text{in2}$, so bilinear layers are usually
kept narrow.  Reduces to two ordinary linear layers when
$W_k = u_k v_k^\top$ is rank-one (the *factorised bilinear*
trick).

members.name: fused_linear_relu
members.signature: fused_linear_relu(x: Tensor, weight: Tensor, bias: Tensor)
members.summary: Fused linear + ReLU forward kernel.
members.extended:
Computes $\text{ReLU}(xW^\top + b)$ in a single CPU pass — the
pre-activation tensor is never materialised, halving memory traffic on
large MLP blocks.  Falls back to the unfused two-op path whenever any
input requires gradient so that autograd remains correct.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input, shape `(..., in_features)`.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description: Weight matrix, shape `(out_features, in_features)`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description: Bias vector, shape `(out_features,)`.

returns.description: `relu(x @ weight.T + bias)`, shape `(..., out_features)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import fused_linear_relu
>>> x = lucid.randn(8, 16)
>>> w = lucid.randn(32, 16)
>>> b = lucid.zeros(32)
>>> fused_linear_relu(x, w, b).shape
(8, 32)
notes:
$$
y = \max\!\big(0,\, x W^\top + b\big)
$$

Implementation: a single BLAS SGEMM followed by vDSP `vrelu` over
the GEMM output buffer.  Selected by the Phase-19 FusionPass when
grad-mode is disabled and none of `x` / `weight` / `bias` has
`requires_grad=True`.  In training mode the call routes through
the standard `linear` + `relu` graph so autograd's
backward derivations are exact.

members.name: fused_linear_gelu
members.signature: fused_linear_gelu(x: Tensor, weight: Tensor, bias: Tensor, approximate: str = 'tanh')
members.summary: Fused linear + GELU forward kernel.
members.extended:
Computes $\text{GELU}(xW^\top + b)$ in a single CPU pass.  Used
extensively in transformer feed-forward blocks where the linear-then-
GELU pattern dominates inference cost; fusing eliminates the
intermediate activation buffer and improves cache behaviour.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input, shape `(..., in_features)`.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description: Weight matrix, shape `(out_features, in_features)`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description: Bias vector, shape `(out_features,)`.

parameters.name: approximate
parameters.description:
GELU approximation.  `"tanh"` (default) is what the fused
kernel implements; `"none"` falls back to the unfused exact-erf
path.

returns.description: `gelu(x @ weight.T + bias)`, shape `(..., out_features)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import fused_linear_gelu
>>> x = lucid.randn(8, 16)
>>> w = lucid.randn(32, 16)
>>> b = lucid.zeros(32)
>>> fused_linear_gelu(x, w, b).shape
(8, 32)
notes:
$$
y = \text{GELU}\!\big(x W^\top + b\big)
$$

Implementation: a single BLAS SGEMM followed by vForce `vtanh`
over the GEMM output to evaluate the tanh-form GELU
$\tfrac{x}{2}\big[1 + \tanh(\sqrt{2/\pi}\,(x + 0.044715 x^3))\big]$.
Engaged only in inference mode (no grad-tracking inputs) and only
when `approximate="tanh"`; otherwise the call falls back to the
standard `linear` + `gelu` graph so gradients remain
exact.

members.name: conv1d
members.signature: conv1d(x: Tensor, weight: Tensor, bias: Tensor | None = None, stride: int | tuple[int, ...] = 1, padding: int | tuple[int, ...] = 0, dilation: int | tuple[int, ...] = 1, groups: int = 1)
members.summary: 1-D cross-correlation over batched 3-D input.
members.extended:
Slides a learned filter along a single spatial axis.  As with every
major deep-learning framework, the operation is technically
cross-correlation rather than strict mathematical convolution (no
kernel flip), but the "conv" name is kept for familiarity.  Channel
mixing happens through the `in_channels` dimension of `weight`.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C_in, L)`.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description: Filters of shape `(C_out, C_in/groups, kL)`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description: Per-output-channel bias of shape `(C_out,)`.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Step between adjacent kernel positions (default `1`).

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description: Zero padding applied to both sides of the spatial axis.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between kernel taps (atrous convolution).  Default `1`.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description: Split the channel dimension into `groups` independent groups.

returns.description:
Output of shape `(N, C_out, L_out)` where



$$
L_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{L + 2p - d(k - 1) - 1}{s} + 1 \right\rfloor
$$

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import conv1d
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 4, 50)
>>> w = lucid.randn(8, 4, 3)
>>> y = conv1d(x, w, padding=1)
>>> y.shape
(2, 8, 50)
notes:
Math:



$$
y_{i,\,c_o,\,l} = b_{c_o} + \sum_{c_i, m} w_{c_o,\,c_i,\,m} \cdot x_{i,\,c_i,\,s l + d m}
$$

Backward is registered automatically; gradients flow to `x`,
`weight`, and `bias`.  Set `groups == C_in` and `C_out == C_in`
for a depthwise convolution.  Using `dilation > 1` enlarges the
receptive field without increasing parameter count — common in
speech / audio models (WaveNet) and 1-D temporal CNNs.

members.name: conv2d
members.signature: conv2d(x: Tensor, weight: Tensor, bias: Tensor | None = None, stride: int | tuple[int, int] = 1, padding: int | tuple[int, int] = 0, dilation: int | tuple[int, int] = 1, groups: int = 1)
members.summary: 2-D cross-correlation over batched 4-D input.
members.extended:
Despite the name, computes **cross-correlation** rather than strict
mathematical convolution (no kernel flip).  The kernel slides over
the input applying a learned linear combination at each spatial
position; channel mixing happens through the `in_channels`
dimension of `weight`.  This is the workhorse op of modern image
CNNs (ResNet, ConvNeXt, EfficientNet, ...).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C_in, H, W)`.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description: Filters of shape `(C_out, C_in/groups, kH, kW)`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description: Per-output-channel bias of shape `(C_out,)`.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Step between adjacent kernel positions (default `1`).

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description: Zero padding on each spatial side.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between kernel taps (atrous convolution).  Default `1`.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description:
Split the channels into `groups` independent groups.  Setting
`groups == C_in` gives a depthwise convolution.

returns.description:
Output of shape `(N, C_out, H_out, W_out)` where



$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{out}} &= \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2 p_H - d_H (k_H - 1) - 1}{s_H} + 1 \right\rfloor \\
W_{\text{out}} &= \left\lfloor \frac{W + 2 p_W - d_W (k_W - 1) - 1}{s_W} + 1 \right\rfloor
\end{aligned}
$$

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import conv2d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 32, 32)
>>> w = lucid.randn(8, 3, 3, 3)
>>> y = conv2d(x, w, stride=1, padding=1)
>>> y.shape
(1, 8, 32, 32)
notes:
Math:



$$
y_{i,\,c_o,\,h,\,w} = b_{c_o} + \sum_{c_i, m, n} w_{c_o,\,c_i,\,m,\,n} \cdot x_{i,\,c_i,\,s_H h + d_H m,\,s_W w + d_W n}
$$

Backward is well-known; gradients w.r.t. `x`, `weight`, and
`bias` flow through automatically.  `groups > 1` yields grouped
convolution (channel-blocks computed independently); `groups ==
C_in` plus `C_out == C_in` is depthwise convolution.  Dilation
enlarges the receptive field without inflating parameter count.

members.name: conv3d
members.signature: conv3d(x: Tensor, weight: Tensor, bias: Tensor | None = None, stride: int | tuple[int, int, int] = 1, padding: int | tuple[int, int, int] = 0, dilation: int | tuple[int, int, int] = 1, groups: int = 1)
members.summary: 3-D cross-correlation over batched 5-D input.
members.extended:
Extends `conv2d` to volumetric data (depth × height × width).
Standard in medical imaging (CT, MRI), video understanding (I3D,
3D-ResNet, SlowFast) and any setting where the input has three
spatial axes.  As with the 1-D and 2-D variants, this is technically
cross-correlation rather than strict convolution.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C_in, D, H, W)`.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description: Filters of shape `(C_out, C_in/groups, kD, kH, kW)`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description: Per-output-channel bias of shape `(C_out,)`.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Step between adjacent kernel positions per axis (default `1`).

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description: Zero padding on each spatial side.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between kernel taps (atrous convolution).  Default `1`.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description: Split channels into `groups` independent groups.

returns.description:
Output of shape `(N, C_out, D_out, H_out, W_out)` where each
spatial size obeys



$$
D_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{D + 2 p_D - d_D (k_D - 1) - 1}{s_D} + 1 \right\rfloor
$$

(analogously for `H` and `W`).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import conv3d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 1, 16, 32, 32)
>>> w = lucid.randn(4, 1, 3, 3, 3)
>>> y = conv3d(x, w, padding=1)
>>> y.shape
(1, 4, 16, 32, 32)
notes:
Math:



$$
y_{i,\,c_o,\,d,\,h,\,w} = b_{c_o} + \sum_{c_i,\,m,\,n,\,p} w_{c_o,\,c_i,\,m,\,n,\,p} \cdot x_{i,\,c_i,\,s_D d + d_D m,\,s_H h + d_H n,\,s_W w + d_W p}
$$

3-D convolution has cubic kernel cost in `k` — for large kernels,
consider factorised variants (e.g. `(1, k, k)` followed by
`(k, 1, 1)`) which trade expressivity for compute.

members.name: conv_transpose1d
members.signature: conv_transpose1d(x: Tensor, weight: Tensor, bias: Tensor | None = None, stride: int | tuple[int, ...] = 1, padding: int | tuple[int, ...] = 0, output_padding: int | tuple[int, ...] = 0, groups: int = 1, dilation: int | tuple[int, ...] = 1)
members.summary: Transposed 1-D convolution (a.k.a. "fractionally-strided" conv).
members.extended:
Often called "deconvolution" in the literature, but this is **not**
the mathematical inverse of `conv1d` — it is the gradient
operator of a forward convolution, used to map a low-resolution
feature map back to a higher-resolution one.  The standard
upsampling primitive in 1-D decoder networks (autoencoders, TTS
vocoders, etc.).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C_in, L_in)`.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description: Filters of shape `(C_in, C_out/groups, kL)`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description:
Per-output-channel bias of shape `(C_out,)`.  When `None`,
a zero bias is materialised internally (the engine op requires
an explicit bias tensor).

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Upsampling factor between input cells (default `1`).

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description: Symmetric zero-padding shaved from each side of the output.

parameters.name: output_padding
parameters.description:
Extra one-sided trailing padding added to the output.  Used to
disambiguate the output size when `stride > 1`.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description: Channel grouping (currently `1`-only in the engine path).

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between kernel taps.

returns.description:
Output of shape `(N, C_out, L_out)` where



$$
L_{\text{out}} = (L_{\text{in}} - 1) \cdot s - 2 p + d (k - 1) + \text{op} + 1
$$

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import conv_transpose1d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 4, 10)
>>> w = lucid.randn(4, 2, 3)     # (C_in, C_out, kL)
>>> y = conv_transpose1d(x, w, stride=2)
>>> y.shape
(1, 2, 21)
notes:
Conceptually, transposed convolution inserts `stride - 1` zeros
between consecutive input samples and then runs a normal
convolution — hence the "fractional stride" name.  In practice the
engine implements it as the matrix-transpose of the corresponding
forward conv, which avoids materialising the zero-stuffed buffer.

Checkerboard artifacts (regular high-frequency patterns) commonly
arise when `kernel_size` is not divisible by `stride`; an
alternative is `conv1d` after upsampling.

members.name: conv_transpose2d
members.signature: conv_transpose2d(x: Tensor, weight: Tensor, bias: Tensor | None = None, stride: int | tuple[int, int] = 1, padding: int | tuple[int, int] = 0, output_padding: int | tuple[int, int] = 0, groups: int = 1, dilation: int | tuple[int, int] = 1)
members.summary: Transposed 2-D convolution — the standard upsampling primitive.
members.extended:
Also referred to as "deconvolution", though this is not the
mathematical inverse of `conv2d`.  Performs the transpose of
a 2-D forward convolution: each input cell scatters a weighted
copy of the kernel into the (larger) output canvas.  Used in
image-segmentation decoders, GAN generators, super-resolution
networks, and U-Net style architectures.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C_in, H_in, W_in)`.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description: Filters of shape `(C_in, C_out/groups, kH, kW)`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description:
Per-output-channel bias of shape `(C_out,)`.  Materialised as
zeros when `None`.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Upsampling factor between input cells (default `1`).

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description: Implicit zero-padding subtracted from each side of the output.

parameters.name: output_padding
parameters.description:
Additional one-sided trailing padding on the output, used to
resolve the size ambiguity when `stride > 1`.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description: Channel grouping.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between kernel taps.

returns.description:
Output of shape `(N, C_out, H_out, W_out)` where each spatial
dimension satisfies



$$
H_{\text{out}} = (H_{\text{in}} - 1) \cdot s_H - 2 p_H + d_H (k_H - 1) + \text{op}_H + 1
$$

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import conv_transpose2d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 16, 7, 7)
>>> w = lucid.randn(16, 8, 4, 4)
>>> y = conv_transpose2d(x, w, stride=2, padding=1)
>>> y.shape
(1, 8, 14, 14)
notes:
Checkerboard artifacts are a well-known failure mode when
`kernel_size` is not a multiple of `stride`.  Alternatives are
nearest/bilinear upsample + `conv2d`, or pixel-shuffle
(sub-pixel convolution).

The forward of this op equals the **backward of conv2d w.r.t.
input**; that duality is what makes it useful as a learnable
upsampler — its gradients give the structure of an ordinary conv.

members.name: conv_transpose3d
members.signature: conv_transpose3d(x: Tensor, weight: Tensor, bias: Tensor | None = None, stride: int | tuple[int, int, int] = 1, padding: int | tuple[int, int, int] = 0, output_padding: int | tuple[int, int, int] = 0, groups: int = 1, dilation: int | tuple[int, int, int] = 1)
members.summary: Transposed 3-D convolution — volumetric upsampling.
members.extended:
Extends `conv_transpose2d` to three spatial dimensions.
Standard in 3-D segmentation decoders (V-Net, 3D-UNet), volumetric
GANs, and video generation.  Like the 1-D / 2-D variants, this is
the matrix-transpose of an ordinary 3-D convolution rather than a
true mathematical inverse.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C_in, D_in, H_in, W_in)`.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description: Filters of shape `(C_in, C_out/groups, kD, kH, kW)`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description:
Per-output-channel bias of shape `(C_out,)`.  Materialised as
zeros when `None`.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Upsampling factor per spatial axis.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description: Implicit zero-padding subtracted from each side of the output.

parameters.name: output_padding
parameters.description: Trailing one-sided output padding.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description: Channel grouping.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between kernel taps.

returns.description:
Output of shape `(N, C_out, D_out, H_out, W_out)` where each
spatial size obeys



$$
D_{\text{out}} = (D_{\text{in}} - 1) \cdot s_D - 2 p_D + d_D (k_D - 1) + \text{op}_D + 1
$$

(analogously for `H` and `W`).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import conv_transpose3d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 8, 4, 8, 8)
>>> w = lucid.randn(8, 4, 2, 2, 2)
>>> y = conv_transpose3d(x, w, stride=2)
>>> y.shape
(1, 4, 8, 16, 16)
notes:
Memory cost scales cubically in the spatial dimensions; for large
volumes a typical pattern is to interleave trilinear upsample with
`conv3d` instead.  Checkerboard artifacts also extend into
three dimensions when `kernel_size` is not a multiple of
`stride`.

members.name: batch_norm
members.signature: batch_norm(x: Tensor, running_mean: Tensor | None, running_var: Tensor | None, weight: Tensor | None = None, bias: Tensor | None = None, training: bool = False, momentum: float = 0.1, eps: float = 1e-05)
members.summary: Batch normalization (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015).
members.extended:
Normalises each channel using statistics computed across the batch
and all spatial axes, then applies a learnable per-channel affine
transform.  Acts as a strong regulariser and an enabler of higher
learning rates by reducing "internal covariate shift".

parameters.name: x
parameters.description:
Input of shape `(N, C, *)` with 2–5 dimensions:
`(N, C)`, `(N, C, L)`, `(N, C, H, W)`, or
`(N, C, D, H, W)`.

parameters.name: running_mean
parameters.description: Running mean buffer of shape `(C,)`; consulted in eval mode.

parameters.name: running_var
parameters.description: Running variance buffer of shape `(C,)`; consulted in eval mode.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description:
Per-channel scale $\gamma$ of shape `(C,)`.  Defaults to
ones (no scaling).

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description:
Per-channel shift $\beta$ of shape `(C,)`.  Defaults to
zeros (no shift).

parameters.name: training
parameters.description:
When `True`, statistics come from the current batch; when
`False` and running buffers are supplied, those are used.

parameters.name: momentum
parameters.description: Exponential-moving-average coefficient for the running buffers.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Numerical safety added inside the square root.

returns.description: Same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import batch_norm
>>> x = lucid.randn(8, 16, 32, 32)
>>> rm = lucid.zeros(16); rv = lucid.ones(16)
>>> y = batch_norm(x, rm, rv, training=True)
>>> y.shape
(8, 16, 32, 32)
notes:
Math (over batch $\mathcal{B}$ and spatial dims):



$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{\mathcal{B}} &= \frac{1}{|\mathcal{B}|} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}} x_i \\
\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 &= \frac{1}{|\mathcal{B}|} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}} (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})^2 \\
\hat{x}_i &= \frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}} \\
y_i &= \gamma\,\hat{x}_i + \beta
\end{aligned}
$$

Dispatches internally by `ndim` to the matching engine kernel:
`ndim==2` is treated as `(N, C, 1)`; `ndim==3` uses
`batch_norm1d`; `ndim==4` uses the 2-D op; `ndim==5` uses
`batch_norm3d`.  Eval mode bypasses statistics computation
entirely and uses `running_mean` / `running_var` directly.

members.name: layer_norm
members.signature: layer_norm(x: Tensor, normalized_shape: list[int] | tuple[int, ...], weight: Tensor | None = None, bias: Tensor | None = None, eps: float = 1e-05)
members.summary: Layer normalization (Ba, Kiros & Hinton, 2016).
members.extended:
Normalises each sample independently across the **last**
`normalized_shape` dimensions.  Unlike `batch_norm`, no
batch statistics are involved — making LayerNorm the default
choice for transformers and other models where batches may be
small or sequence lengths variable.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input whose trailing dims match `normalized_shape`.

parameters.name: normalized_shape
parameters.annotation: list of int or tuple of int
parameters.description:
Trailing dims to normalise over.  E.g. `(d,)` for a
token-wise normalisation in a transformer with hidden size
`d`.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description:
Per-element scale $\gamma$ of shape `normalized_shape`.
Defaults to ones.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description:
Per-element shift $\beta$ of shape `normalized_shape`.
Defaults to zeros.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Numerical safety added inside the square root.

returns.description: Same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import layer_norm
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 10, 64)
>>> y = layer_norm(x, normalized_shape=(64,))
>>> y.shape
(2, 10, 64)
notes:
Math (reduction taken over the last $k$ axes,
$k = \mathrm{len}(\text{normalized\_shape})$):



$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \frac{1}{|S|}\sum_{j \in S} x_j \\
\sigma^2 &= \frac{1}{|S|}\sum_{j \in S} (x_j - \mu)^2 \\
y &= \gamma \cdot \frac{x - \mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon}} + \beta
\end{aligned}
$$

Because the reduction is per-sample, behaviour is identical at
train and eval time — no running statistics needed.  This is what
makes LayerNorm so prevalent in sequence models (RNNs,
transformers).

members.name: group_norm
members.signature: group_norm(x: Tensor, num_groups: int, weight: Tensor | None = None, bias: Tensor | None = None, eps: float = 1e-05)
members.summary: Group normalization (Wu & He, 2018).
members.extended:
Splits the channel dimension into `num_groups` contiguous groups
and normalises each `(sample, group)` slice independently across
its channels and spatial axes.  Combines the spatial reduction of
BatchNorm with the per-sample stability of LayerNorm — performance
is therefore largely independent of batch size, which makes it the
go-to choice for detection / segmentation models trained with very
small batches.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description:
Input of shape `(N, C, *spatial)` where `C` must be
divisible by `num_groups`.

parameters.name: num_groups
parameters.description:
Number of channel groups.  Two limiting cases: `num_groups ==
C` reduces to InstanceNorm; `num_groups == 1` reduces to
LayerNorm over channels + spatial axes.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description: Per-channel scale $\gamma$ of shape `(C,)`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description: Per-channel shift $\beta$ of shape `(C,)`.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Numerical safety added inside the square root.

returns.description: Same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import group_norm
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 32, 16, 16)
>>> y = group_norm(x, num_groups=8)
>>> y.shape
(2, 32, 16, 16)
notes:
Math (let $G_g$ be the channel set of group $g$ and
$S$ the spatial axes):



$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{n,g} &= \frac{1}{|G_g||S|} \sum_{c \in G_g} \sum_{s \in S} x_{n,c,s} \\
\sigma^2_{n,g} &= \frac{1}{|G_g||S|} \sum_{c \in G_g} \sum_{s \in S} (x_{n,c,s} - \mu_{n,g})^2 \\
y_{n,c,s} &= \gamma_c \cdot \frac{x_{n,c,s} - \mu_{n,g(c)}}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{n,g(c)} + \epsilon}} + \beta_c
\end{aligned}
$$

Independence from batch size avoids the train/eval mismatch that
BatchNorm requires running buffers to fix.

members.name: rms_norm
members.signature: rms_norm(x: Tensor, normalized_shape: list[int] | tuple[int, ...], weight: Tensor | None = None, eps: float = 1e-08)
members.summary: Root-mean-square layer normalization (Zhang & Sennrich, 2019).
members.extended:
A simplified variant of `layer_norm` that drops mean
centering and the additive bias.  Cheaper to compute and shown to
perform competitively in large language models (LLaMA, T5,
PaLM, ...), where its lower latency and memory footprint matter at
scale.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input whose last dimension matches `normalized_shape`.

parameters.name: normalized_shape
parameters.annotation: list of int or tuple of int
parameters.description:
Trailing dims to normalise over.  Typically `(d,)` where
`d` is the model hidden size.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description: Per-element scale $\gamma$.  Defaults to ones.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Numerical safety added inside the square root.

returns.description: Same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import rms_norm
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 128, 512)
>>> y = rms_norm(x, normalized_shape=(512,))
>>> y.shape
(2, 128, 512)
notes:
Math:



$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RMS}(x) &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{|S|}\sum_{j \in S} x_j^2 + \epsilon} \\
y &= \gamma \cdot \frac{x}{\text{RMS}(x)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The dropped mean-centering step costs LayerNorm one extra reduction
and a subtraction; RMSNorm trades that for a small amount of
expressivity (no shift invariance).  In practice the loss is
negligible at scale.

members.name: instance_norm
members.signature: instance_norm(x: Tensor, running_mean: Tensor | None = None, running_var: Tensor | None = None, weight: Tensor | None = None, bias: Tensor | None = None, use_input_stats: bool = True, momentum: float = 0.1, eps: float = 1e-05)
members.summary: Instance normalization (Ulyanov, Vedaldi & Lempitsky, 2016).
members.extended:
Normalises each `(sample, channel)` slice against its **own**
spatial mean and variance — i.e. statistics are reduced only over
the spatial axes.  Originally introduced for fast neural style
transfer; widely used wherever per-image contrast should be made
invariant (image generation, image-to-image translation).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C, *spatial)` with `ndim >= 3`.

parameters.name: running_mean
parameters.description:
Running per-channel mean of shape `(C,)`.  Only consulted
when `use_input_stats=False`.

parameters.name: running_var
parameters.description: Running per-channel variance of shape `(C,)`.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description: Per-channel scale $\gamma$ of shape `(C,)`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description: Per-channel shift $\beta$ of shape `(C,)`.

parameters.name: use_input_stats
parameters.description:
When `True` (default) statistics come from the current
instance.  When `False` and running buffers are supplied,
those are used instead.

parameters.name: momentum
parameters.description:
EMA coefficient for the running buffers (when externally
updated).

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Numerical safety added inside the square root.

returns.description: Same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import instance_norm
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 3, 64, 64)
>>> y = instance_norm(x)
>>> y.shape
(4, 3, 64, 64)
notes:
Math (reduction taken only over spatial axes $S$):



$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{n,c} &= \frac{1}{|S|}\sum_{s \in S} x_{n,c,s} \\
\sigma^2_{n,c} &= \frac{1}{|S|}\sum_{s \in S} (x_{n,c,s} - \mu_{n,c})^2 \\
y_{n,c,s} &= \gamma_c \cdot \frac{x_{n,c,s} - \mu_{n,c}}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{n,c} + \epsilon}} + \beta_c
\end{aligned}
$$

InstanceNorm equals `group_norm` with `num_groups == C`.
The lack of batch coupling also makes it the natural normaliser
when adapting per-sample statistics matters more than population
coherence — exactly the case in style transfer.

members.name: local_response_norm
members.signature: local_response_norm(x: Tensor, size: int, alpha: float = 0.0001, beta: float = 0.75, k: float = 1.0)
members.summary: Local response normalization (Krizhevsky, Sutskever & Hinton, 2012).
members.extended:
Implements lateral inhibition: each activation is divided by a
function of the squared activations of its channel neighbours.
Introduced with AlexNet and largely superseded by batch / layer
normalisation, but still used in some classic architectures.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description:
Input of shape `(N, C, *spatial)` (any number of spatial
dims).  Inputs with fewer than 2 dimensions are returned
unchanged.

parameters.name: size
parameters.description:
Number of neighbouring channels (the size of the local window)
used in the normalisation.

parameters.name: alpha
parameters.description:
Multiplicative scaling factor on the squared-activation sum.
Default `1e-4`.

parameters.name: beta
parameters.description: Exponent applied to the normalising factor.  Default `0.75`.

parameters.name: k
parameters.description: Additive constant guarding the division.  Default `1.0`.

returns.description: Same shape as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import local_response_norm
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 96, 27, 27)   # AlexNet conv1 output shape
>>> y = local_response_norm(x, size=5, alpha=1e-4, beta=0.75, k=2.0)
>>> y.shape
(1, 96, 27, 27)
notes:
Math (the neighbourhood $\mathcal{N}(c)$ is the `size`
channels centred at $c$, zero-padded at boundaries):



$$
y_{i,c,h,w} = \frac{x_{i,c,h,w}}{\left(k + \alpha \sum_{c' \in \mathcal{N}(c)} x_{i,c',h,w}^2 \right)^\beta}
$$

Boundary handling uses asymmetric zero padding when `size` is
even: `(size - 1) // 2` on the left and `size // 2` on the
right, matching the original AlexNet / cuDNN convention.  LRN
predates BatchNorm and provides only lateral inhibition (no learnt
affine), which is why modern architectures usually do not include
it.

members.name: max_pool1d
members.signature: max_pool1d(x: Tensor, kernel_size: int | tuple[int, ...], stride: int | tuple[int, ...] | None = None, padding: int | tuple[int, ...] = 0, dilation: int | tuple[int, ...] = 1, return_indices: bool = False, ceil_mode: bool = False)
members.summary: 1-D max pooling over a sliding window.
members.extended:
For each window, takes the maximum value — a translation-invariant
feature aggregator that also provides a degree of non-linearity.
Standard ingredient of 1-D temporal CNNs (text, audio, sensor data).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C, L)`.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the pooling window.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Window step.  Defaults to `kernel_size` (non-overlapping).

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description: Implicit zero-padding on both sides of the spatial axis.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between window elements.  Default `1`.

parameters.name: return_indices
parameters.description:
Currently must be `False`; the engine pool op does not yet
emit per-window argmax indices.

parameters.name: ceil_mode
parameters.description:
When `True`, use ceil instead of floor in the output-size
formula.

returns.description:
Output of shape `(N, C, L_out)` where



$$
L_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{L + 2 p - d(k - 1) - 1}{s} + 1 \right\rfloor
$$

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import max_pool1d
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 4, 16)
>>> y = max_pool1d(x, kernel_size=2)
>>> y.shape
(2, 4, 8)
notes:
Math:



$$
y_{i,c,l} = \max_{m = 0, \ldots, k-1} x_{i,\,c,\,s l + d m - p}
$$

Max-pool is sub-differentiable: the gradient flows only through
the position(s) holding the maximum in each window.

members.name: max_pool2d
members.signature: max_pool2d(x: Tensor, kernel_size: int | tuple[int, int], stride: int | tuple[int, int] | None = None, padding: int | tuple[int, int] = 0, dilation: int | tuple[int, int] = 1, return_indices: bool = False, ceil_mode: bool = False)
members.summary: 2-D max pooling over a sliding window.
members.extended:
Aggregates each spatial neighbourhood into its maximum value.
The canonical downsampling primitive in classical image CNNs —
provides invariance to small local shifts and reduces both spatial
extent and compute for subsequent layers.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C, H, W)`.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the pooling window.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Window step.  Defaults to `kernel_size` (non-overlapping).

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description: Implicit zero-padding on each spatial side.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between window elements.  Default `1`.

parameters.name: return_indices
parameters.description: Must currently be `False` — see `max_pool1d`.

parameters.name: ceil_mode
