parameters.description: Use ceil instead of floor in the output-size formula.

returns.description:
Output of shape `(N, C, H_out, W_out)` where each dim obeys



$$
H_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2 p_H - d_H (k_H - 1) - 1}{s_H} + 1 \right\rfloor
$$

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import max_pool2d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 16, 32, 32)
>>> y = max_pool2d(x, kernel_size=2, stride=2)
>>> y.shape
(1, 16, 16, 16)
notes:
Math:



$$
y_{i,c,h,w} = \max_{m,n} x_{i,\,c,\,s_H h + m,\,s_W w + n}
$$

Pure max is sub-differentiable; gradient routes to the per-window
argmax position only.  Empirically the strongest pooling operator
for classification tasks (preserves high-magnitude features).

members.name: max_pool3d
members.signature: max_pool3d(x: Tensor, kernel_size: int | tuple[int, int, int], stride: int | tuple[int, int, int] | None = None, padding: int | tuple[int, int, int] = 0, dilation: int | tuple[int, int, int] = 1, return_indices: bool = False, ceil_mode: bool = False)
members.summary: 3-D max pooling over a sliding window.
members.extended:
Volumetric downsampling primitive — extends `max_pool2d` by
one depth dimension.  Used in 3-D CNNs for medical imaging and
video understanding.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C, D, H, W)`.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the pooling window per axis.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Window step.  Defaults to `kernel_size`.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description: Implicit zero-padding on each spatial side.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between window elements.  Default `1`.

parameters.name: return_indices
parameters.description: Must currently be `False`.

parameters.name: ceil_mode
parameters.description: Use ceil instead of floor in the output-size formula.

returns.description:
Output of shape `(N, C, D_out, H_out, W_out)` where each
spatial dim obeys



$$
D_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{D + 2 p_D - d_D (k_D - 1) - 1}{s_D} + 1 \right\rfloor
$$

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import max_pool3d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 4, 8, 16, 16)
>>> y = max_pool3d(x, kernel_size=2)
>>> y.shape
(1, 4, 4, 8, 8)
notes:
Math:



$$
y_{i,c,d,h,w} = \max_{l,m,n} x_{i,\,c,\,s_D d + l,\,s_H h + m,\,s_W w + n}
$$

Memory cost can become significant for large volumes — when the
network only needs a global summary, prefer
`adaptive_max_pool3d` with `output_size=(1, 1, 1)`.

members.name: avg_pool1d
members.signature: avg_pool1d(x: Tensor, kernel_size: int | tuple[int, ...], stride: int | tuple[int, ...] | None = None, padding: int | tuple[int, ...] = 0, ceil_mode: bool = False, count_include_pad: bool = True)
members.summary: 1-D average pooling over a sliding window.
members.extended:
Replaces each window with its arithmetic mean.  Smoother than
max-pool and used wherever maintaining the overall response
magnitude matters (e.g. before a fully connected classifier head).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C, L)`.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the pooling window.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Window step.  Defaults to `kernel_size` (non-overlapping).

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description: Implicit zero-padding on both sides.

parameters.name: ceil_mode
parameters.description: Use ceil instead of floor in the output-size formula.

parameters.name: count_include_pad
parameters.description: When `True`, padding cells contribute to the denominator.

returns.description:
Output of shape `(N, C, L_out)` where



$$
L_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{L + 2 p - k}{s} + 1 \right\rfloor
$$

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import avg_pool1d
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 20)
>>> y = avg_pool1d(x, kernel_size=4, stride=2)
>>> y.shape
(2, 3, 9)
notes:
Math (averaging window $R$ of cardinality $|R|$):



$$
y_{i,c,l} = \frac{1}{|R|} \sum_{m \in R} x_{i,\,c,\,s l + m - p}
$$

Unlike max-pool, average-pool is fully differentiable everywhere,
so the gradient is uniformly distributed back across all window
positions.

members.name: avg_pool2d
members.signature: avg_pool2d(x: Tensor, kernel_size: int | tuple[int, int], stride: int | tuple[int, int] | None = None, padding: int | tuple[int, int] = 0, ceil_mode: bool = False, count_include_pad: bool = True, divisor_override: int | None = None)
members.summary: 2-D average pooling over a sliding window.
members.extended:
Replaces each spatial window with its arithmetic mean.  Common as
the last spatial reduction before a fully connected classifier
(modern nets often use `adaptive_avg_pool2d` for the final
pool because it adapts to arbitrary input sizes).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C, H, W)`.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the pooling window.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Window step.  Defaults to `kernel_size`.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description: Implicit zero-padding on each spatial side.

parameters.name: ceil_mode
parameters.description: Use ceil instead of floor in the output-size formula.

parameters.name: count_include_pad
parameters.description: When `True`, padding cells contribute to the denominator.

parameters.name: divisor_override
parameters.description:
Explicit denominator overriding `|R|` (rare; useful for
symmetric / weight-style averages).

returns.description:
Output of shape `(N, C, H_out, W_out)` where each dim obeys



$$
H_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2 p_H - k_H}{s_H} + 1 \right\rfloor
$$

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import avg_pool2d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 8, 28, 28)
>>> y = avg_pool2d(x, kernel_size=2)
>>> y.shape
(1, 8, 14, 14)
notes:
Math:



$$
y_{i,c,h,w} = \frac{1}{|R|} \sum_{(m, n) \in R} x_{i,\,c,\,s_H h + m,\,s_W w + n}
$$

Average-pool's gradient is the uniform distribution $1/|R|$
over the window, which produces smoother backward signals than
max-pool.

members.name: avg_pool3d
members.signature: avg_pool3d(x: Tensor, kernel_size: int | tuple[int, int, int], stride: int | tuple[int, int, int] | None = None, padding: int | tuple[int, int, int] = 0, ceil_mode: bool = False, count_include_pad: bool = True, divisor_override: int | None = None)
members.summary: 3-D average pooling over a sliding window.
members.extended:
Volumetric counterpart of `avg_pool2d` — replaces each 3-D
window with its arithmetic mean.  Useful in 3-D CNNs as a smoother
alternative to `max_pool3d`, and as the final "global"
reduction before a classifier head.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C, D, H, W)`.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the pooling window per axis.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Window step.  Defaults to `kernel_size`.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description: Implicit zero-padding on each spatial side.

parameters.name: ceil_mode
parameters.description: Use ceil instead of floor in the output-size formula.

parameters.name: count_include_pad
parameters.description: Whether padding cells contribute to the denominator.

parameters.name: divisor_override
parameters.description: Explicit denominator overriding `|R|`.

returns.description:
Output of shape `(N, C, D_out, H_out, W_out)` where each
spatial dim obeys



$$
D_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{D + 2 p_D - k_D}{s_D} + 1 \right\rfloor
$$

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import avg_pool3d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 4, 8, 16, 16)
>>> y = avg_pool3d(x, kernel_size=2)
>>> y.shape
(1, 4, 4, 8, 8)
notes:
Math:



$$
y_{i,c,d,h,w} = \frac{1}{|R|}
    \sum_{(l, m, n) \in R} x_{i,\,c,\,s_D d + l,\,s_H h + m,\,s_W w + n}
$$

Average-pool's gradient is uniformly distributed across the
window, so it produces smoother backward signals than its
max-based counterpart.

members.name: adaptive_avg_pool1d
members.signature: adaptive_avg_pool1d(x: Tensor, output_size: int | tuple[int, ...])
members.summary: 1-D adaptive average pooling — produces a fixed output length.
members.extended:
Computes kernel / stride dynamically so that the output's spatial
length equals `output_size` regardless of input length.  Handy
when feeding variable-length sequences into a head expecting a
fixed embedding size.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C, L)`.

parameters.name: output_size
parameters.description: Desired length of the output spatial axis.

returns.description: Output of shape `(N, C, output_size)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import adaptive_avg_pool1d
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 32, 13)
>>> y = adaptive_avg_pool1d(x, output_size=5)
>>> y.shape
(2, 32, 5)
notes:
For each output index $i$, the corresponding input window is



$$
\big[\lfloor i \cdot L / L_{\text{out}} \rfloor,\;
      \lceil (i+1) \cdot L / L_{\text{out}} \rceil\big)
$$

and the cell is the mean over that window.  When `L` divides
evenly by `L_out` the operation reduces to plain
`avg_pool1d` with kernel == stride == `L / L_out`;
otherwise Lucid falls back to a per-slot Python-level computation
that still keeps all data on the original device.

members.name: adaptive_avg_pool2d
members.signature: adaptive_avg_pool2d(x: Tensor, output_size: int | tuple[int, int])
members.summary: 2-D adaptive average pooling — produces a fixed `(H, W)`.
members.extended:
Computes per-axis kernel / stride so the output shape exactly
matches `output_size` regardless of the input spatial size.  The
workhorse "global pool to fixed grid" used by virtually every
modern classification network (`output_size=(1, 1)` collapses
the spatial axes entirely).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C, H, W)`.

parameters.name: output_size
parameters.description:
Desired spatial shape of the output.  Scalar duplicates per
axis; `(1, 1)` performs global average pooling.

returns.description: Output of shape `(N, C, oH, oW)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import adaptive_avg_pool2d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 512, 7, 9)
>>> y = adaptive_avg_pool2d(x, output_size=(1, 1))
>>> y.shape
(1, 512, 1, 1)
notes:
For each output cell $(i, j)$ the corresponding input
window is



$$
\big[\lfloor i H / H_{\text{out}} \rfloor,
      \lceil (i + 1) H / H_{\text{out}} \rceil\big) \times
\big[\lfloor j W / W_{\text{out}} \rfloor,
      \lceil (j + 1) W / W_{\text{out}} \rceil\big)
$$

Foundational to Spatial Pyramid Pooling and the "global average
pool + linear classifier" pattern introduced by NIN /
ResNet-style architectures.

members.name: adaptive_avg_pool3d
members.signature: adaptive_avg_pool3d(x: Tensor, output_size: int | tuple[int, int, int])
members.summary: 3-D adaptive average pooling — produces a fixed `(D, H, W)`.
members.extended:
Volumetric counterpart of `adaptive_avg_pool2d`.  Used by
3-D classification heads to collapse a volumetric feature map to
a fixed embedding shape regardless of input size.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C, D, H, W)`.

parameters.name: output_size
parameters.description: Desired output spatial shape.

returns.description: Output of shape `(N, C, oD, oH, oW)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import adaptive_avg_pool3d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 16, 8, 10, 10)
>>> y = adaptive_avg_pool3d(x, output_size=(2, 2, 2))
>>> y.shape
(1, 16, 2, 2, 2)
notes:
Per-cell window definitions follow the same floor / ceil
construction used in the 2-D variant.  The cell value is the mean
over the volumetric window:



$$
y_{n,c,d,h,w} = \frac{1}{|R|}
    \sum_{(p, q, r) \in R(d, h, w)} x_{n,\,c,\,p,\,q,\,r}
$$

When the input dims divide `output_size` evenly the engine path
is taken; otherwise a per-slot Python fallback runs (all data
stays on-device).

members.name: adaptive_max_pool2d
members.signature: adaptive_max_pool2d(x: Tensor, output_size: int | tuple[int, int], return_indices: bool = False)
members.summary: 2-D adaptive max pooling — fixed-shape `(H, W)` via per-cell max.
members.extended:
Like `adaptive_avg_pool2d` but takes the maximum over each
dynamically computed window instead of the mean.  Useful when
salient peaks should drive the downstream representation
(e.g. object detectors that rely on strong activations).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C, H, W)`.

parameters.name: output_size
parameters.description: Desired spatial output shape.

parameters.name: return_indices
parameters.description: Must currently be `False`.

returns.description: Output of shape `(N, C, oH, oW)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import adaptive_max_pool2d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 64, 11, 13)
>>> y = adaptive_max_pool2d(x, output_size=(3, 3))
>>> y.shape
(1, 64, 3, 3)
notes:
For each output cell, the window definition is the same as in
`adaptive_avg_pool2d`; the cell value is



$$
y_{i,c,h,w} = \max_{(m, n) \in R(h, w)} x_{i,\,c,\,m,\,n}
$$

Gradient flows only through the per-window argmax position.

members.name: adaptive_max_pool1d
members.signature: adaptive_max_pool1d(x: Tensor, output_size: int | tuple[int, ...], return_indices: bool = False)
members.summary: 1-D adaptive max pooling — produces a fixed output length.
members.extended:
Computes kernel / stride dynamically so the output length equals
`output_size` regardless of input length, then takes the
per-window maximum.  Common in NLP / audio CNNs that feed
fixed-size embeddings into a head.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C, L)`.

parameters.name: output_size
parameters.description: Desired output length.

parameters.name: return_indices
parameters.description: Must currently be `False`.

returns.description: Output of shape `(N, C, output_size)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import adaptive_max_pool1d
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 8, 21)
>>> y = adaptive_max_pool1d(x, output_size=4)
>>> y.shape
(2, 8, 4)
notes:
The window for output index $i$ is



$$
\big[\lfloor i L / L_{\text{out}} \rfloor,\;
      \lceil (i+1) L / L_{\text{out}} \rceil\big)
$$

and the cell takes the max over that window.  Equivalent to
`max_pool1d` with kernel == stride == `L / L_out` when the
division is exact.

members.name: adaptive_max_pool3d
members.signature: adaptive_max_pool3d(x: Tensor, output_size: int | tuple[int, int, int], return_indices: bool = False)
members.summary: 3-D adaptive max pooling — produces a fixed `(D, H, W)`.
members.extended:
Volumetric analogue of `adaptive_max_pool2d`.  Computes
per-axis kernel and stride so the output spatial shape exactly
matches `output_size` regardless of input shape.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C, D, H, W)`.

parameters.name: output_size
parameters.description: Desired output spatial shape.

parameters.name: return_indices
parameters.description: Must currently be `False`.

returns.description: Output of shape `(N, C, oD, oH, oW)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import adaptive_max_pool3d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 8, 5, 7, 9)
>>> y = adaptive_max_pool3d(x, output_size=(1, 1, 1))
>>> y.shape
(1, 8, 1, 1, 1)
notes:
The window for output cell $(i, j, k)$ is the 3-way product
of axis-wise floor / ceil intervals (see
`adaptive_avg_pool2d` for the 2-D version).  The cell takes
the maximum over that volume:



$$
y_{n,c,d,h,w} = \max_{(p, q, r) \in R(d, h, w)} x_{n,\,c,\,p,\,q,\,r}
$$

members.name: lp_pool1d
members.signature: lp_pool1d(x: Tensor, norm_type: float, kernel_size: int | tuple[int], stride: int | tuple[int] | None = None, ceil_mode: bool = False)
members.summary: 1-D Lp-norm pooling — $\big(\sum |x|^p\big)^{1/p}$.
members.extended:
Generalises average and max pooling under a single parameter
$p = \text{norm\_type}$.  $p = 1$ recovers
sum-pooling (= `|R|` × `avg_pool1d`); $p = 2$ is
energy / RMS-style pooling; $p \to \infty$ approaches
`max_pool1d`.  Provides a smooth, fully differentiable bridge
between the two extremes.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C, L)`.

parameters.name: norm_type
parameters.description: Exponent $p > 0$.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the pooling window.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Window step.  Defaults to `kernel_size`.

parameters.name: ceil_mode
parameters.description: Use ceil instead of floor in the output-size formula.

returns.description: Output of shape `(N, C, L_out)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import lp_pool1d
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 4, 12)
>>> y = lp_pool1d(x, norm_type=2.0, kernel_size=2)
>>> y.shape
(2, 4, 6)
notes:
Math (per window $R$):



$$
y_{i,c,l} = \left( \sum_{m \in R} |x_{i,c,m}|^p \right)^{1/p}
$$

Implemented as `(avg_pool1d(|x|^p) · |R|)^(1/p)` so the entire
operator inherits its gradient from the average-pool engine path.

members.name: lp_pool2d
members.signature: lp_pool2d(x: Tensor, norm_type: float, kernel_size: int | tuple[int, int], stride: int | tuple[int, int] | None = None, ceil_mode: bool = False)
members.summary: 2-D Lp-norm pooling — $\big(\sum |x|^p\big)^{1/p}$.
members.extended:
Two-dimensional version of `lp_pool1d`.  The exponent
$p$ interpolates between sum / average pooling (small
$p$) and max pooling (large $p$), giving the network
a tunable "softness" parameter.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C, H, W)`.

parameters.name: norm_type
parameters.description: Exponent $p > 0$.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the pooling window.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Window step.  Defaults to `kernel_size`.

parameters.name: ceil_mode
parameters.description: Use ceil instead of floor in the output-size formula.

returns.description: Output of shape `(N, C, H_out, W_out)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import lp_pool2d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 8, 16, 16)
>>> y = lp_pool2d(x, norm_type=3.0, kernel_size=2)
>>> y.shape
(1, 8, 8, 8)
notes:
Math (per window $R$):



$$
y_{i,c,h,w} = \left( \sum_{(m, n) \in R} |x_{i,c,m,n}|^p \right)^{1/p}
$$

The operator is fully differentiable for any $p > 0$.

members.name: lp_pool3d
members.signature: lp_pool3d(x: Tensor, norm_type: float, kernel_size: int | tuple[int, int, int], stride: int | tuple[int, int, int] | None = None, ceil_mode: bool = False)
members.summary: 3-D Lp-norm pooling — $\big(\sum |x|^p\big)^{1/p}$.
members.extended:
Volumetric version of `lp_pool2d`.  Interpolates between
sum / average pooling (small $p$) and max pooling (large
$p$) over a 3-D window.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C, D, H, W)`.

parameters.name: norm_type
parameters.description: Exponent $p > 0$.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the pooling window per axis.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Window step.  Defaults to `kernel_size`.

parameters.name: ceil_mode
parameters.description: Use ceil instead of floor in the output-size formula.

returns.description: Output of shape `(N, C, D_out, H_out, W_out)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import lp_pool3d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 2, 8, 8, 8)
>>> y = lp_pool3d(x, norm_type=2.0, kernel_size=2)
>>> y.shape
(1, 2, 4, 4, 4)
notes:
Math (per window $R$):



$$
y_{i,c,d,h,w} = \left( \sum_{(l, m, n) \in R} |x_{i,c,l,m,n}|^p \right)^{1/p}
$$

members.name: max_unpool1d
members.signature: max_unpool1d(x: Tensor, indices: Tensor, kernel_size: int | tuple[int], stride: int | tuple[int] | None = None, padding: int | tuple[int] = 0, output_size: tuple[int, ...] | None = None)
members.summary: Inverse of `max_pool1d` via scatter at saved argmax indices.
members.extended:
Restores a "sparse" higher-resolution feature map by placing each
value of `x` at the spatial position recorded in `indices`
(i.e. the argmax positions captured by the corresponding max-pool
forward pass).  Non-selected positions remain zero.  Standard
component of encoder-decoder networks such as SegNet that
propagate pooling indices through skip connections.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Pooled values, shape `(N, C, L_in)`.

parameters.name: indices
parameters.description:
Per-window argmax positions (flattened spatial coordinate),
same shape as `x`.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description:
Original pooling window size (recorded for symmetry — not used
directly in the scatter math).

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Original stride.  Recorded for symmetry.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description: Original padding.  Recorded for symmetry.

parameters.name: output_size
parameters.description:
Required.  Target spatial shape — typically the length of the
tensor that was originally fed to `max_pool1d`.

returns.description:
Output of shape `(N, C, L_out)` containing `x` scattered
into a zero buffer at the positions recorded in `indices`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import max_unpool1d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 2, 4)
>>> idx = lucid.tensor([[[0, 2, 4, 6], [1, 3, 5, 7]]], dtype=lucid.int64)
>>> y = max_unpool1d(x, idx, kernel_size=2, output_size=(8,))
>>> y.shape
(1, 2, 8)
notes:
Math (let $\phi$ be the bijection from pooled-cell index to
saved flat input position):



$$
y_{n,c,\phi(n,c,l)} = x_{n,c,l},\qquad y_{n,c,j} = 0 \text{ otherwise}
$$

Implemented as a differentiable `scatter_add` over a
flattened spatial axis — gradients flow back to `x`.
`output_size` is required because Lucid does not yet emit
argmax indices from the forward pool, so we cannot infer the
original spatial extent.

members.name: max_unpool2d
members.signature: max_unpool2d(x: Tensor, indices: Tensor, kernel_size: int | tuple[int, int], stride: int | tuple[int, int] | None = None, padding: int | tuple[int, int] = 0, output_size: tuple[int, ...] | None = None)
members.summary: Inverse of `max_pool2d` via scatter at saved argmax indices.
members.extended:
Two-dimensional version of `max_unpool1d`.  Scatters the
pooled values back to their original `(H, W)` positions
(recorded as flat `H × W` indices) on a zero canvas.  Standard
in SegNet-style decoders where decoder layers reuse pooling
indices from the encoder for shape-preserving up-sampling.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Pooled values, shape `(N, C, H_in, W_in)`.

parameters.name: indices
parameters.description:
Flattened argmax positions (`row * W + col`), same shape as
`x`.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Original pooling window size.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Original stride.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description: Original padding.

parameters.name: output_size
parameters.description:
Required.  Target spatial shape `(..., H_out, W_out)` — the
original input shape to the matching max-pool.

returns.description: Output of shape `(N, C, H_out, W_out)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import max_unpool2d, max_pool2d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 1, 4, 4)
>>> # forward pool (return_indices currently not supported by engine):
>>> # idx must be supplied by the user when round-tripping.
>>> pooled = max_pool2d(x, kernel_size=2)
>>> idx = lucid.zeros_like(pooled, dtype=lucid.int64)
>>> y = max_unpool2d(pooled, idx, kernel_size=2, output_size=(4, 4))
>>> y.shape
(1, 1, 4, 4)
notes:
Implemented via `scatter_add` over a flattened spatial axis,
fully differentiable.  See `max_unpool1d` for the math.

members.name: max_unpool3d
members.signature: max_unpool3d(x: Tensor, indices: Tensor, kernel_size: int | tuple[int, int, int], stride: int | tuple[int, int, int] | None = None, padding: int | tuple[int, int, int] = 0, output_size: tuple[int, ...] | None = None)
members.summary: Inverse of `max_pool3d` via scatter at saved argmax indices.
members.extended:
Volumetric version of `max_unpool2d`.  Scatters the pooled
values back to their saved `(D, H, W)` positions (encoded as
flat `D × H × W` indices) on a zero canvas.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Pooled values, shape `(N, C, D_in, H_in, W_in)`.

parameters.name: indices
parameters.description: Flattened argmax positions, same shape as `x`.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Original pooling window size.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Original stride.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description: Original padding.

parameters.name: output_size
parameters.description: Required.  Target spatial shape `(..., D_out, H_out, W_out)`.

returns.description: Output of shape `(N, C, D_out, H_out, W_out)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import max_unpool3d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 1, 2, 2, 2)
>>> idx = lucid.zeros_like(x, dtype=lucid.int64)
>>> y = max_unpool3d(x, idx, kernel_size=2, output_size=(4, 4, 4))
>>> y.shape
(1, 1, 4, 4, 4)
notes:
Implemented via `scatter_add` over a flattened spatial axis
(so the gradient flows back to `x` and `indices` is treated
as a non-differentiable lookup).  See `max_unpool1d` for
the math.

members.name: fractional_max_pool2d
members.signature: fractional_max_pool2d(x: Tensor, kernel_size: int | tuple[int, int], output_size: int | tuple[int, int] | None = None, output_ratio: float | tuple[float, float] | None = None, return_indices: bool = False, _random_samples: Tensor | None = None)
members.summary: Fractional max-pooling over a 2-D input (Graham, 2014).
members.extended:
Performs max-pooling with a **non-integer** effective stride.
Instead of a uniform window grid, random per-axis offsets pick
"slightly irregular" window boundaries, yielding a smoother
downsampling than the abrupt 2× reductions of standard max-pool.
Acts as a structural regulariser for image classifiers.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C, H, W)`.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the pooling window.

parameters.name: output_size
parameters.description:
Exact desired output spatial shape.  Mutually exclusive with
`output_ratio`.

parameters.name: output_ratio
parameters.description:
Multiplicative shrinkage factor per axis (e.g. `0.5` halves
each dim).  Mutually exclusive with `output_size`.

parameters.name: return_indices
parameters.description:
When `True`, also return per-output-cell argmax positions
(flattened `H × W` index) as a second tensor.

parameters.name: _random_samples
parameters.description:
Per-instance uniform samples of shape `(N, C, 2)` driving
the window-boundary draws.  Defaults to fresh
`lucid.rand`.

returns.description:
Output of shape `(N, C, oH, oW)`; when `return_indices`,
also the argmax indices of matching shape.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import fractional_max_pool2d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 4, 14, 14)
>>> y = fractional_max_pool2d(x, kernel_size=2, output_ratio=0.5)
>>> y.shape
(1, 4, 7, 7)
notes:
Window starts are drawn from Graham's formula (§3 of the paper)



$$
\alpha = \frac{H - k}{H_{\text{out}} - 1},\quad
\text{start}_i = \lfloor (i + u) \alpha \rfloor - \lfloor u \alpha \rfloor
$$

where $u$ is a per-(sample, channel) uniform sample.  The
final start clamps to `H - k` so all windows lie inside the
input.  Implementation is a pure-Python composite — gradients
flow through `Tensor.max`.

members.name: fractional_max_pool3d
members.signature: fractional_max_pool3d(x: Tensor, kernel_size: int | tuple[int, int, int], output_size: int | tuple[int, int, int] | None = None, output_ratio: float | tuple[float, float, float] | None = None, return_indices: bool = False, _random_samples: Tensor | None = None)
members.summary: Fractional max-pooling over a 3-D input (Graham, 2014).
members.extended:
Volumetric extension of `fractional_max_pool2d`.  Provides
non-integer effective stride along all three spatial axes — a
structural regulariser for 3-D CNNs.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape `(N, C, D, H, W)`.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the pooling window per axis.

parameters.name: output_size
parameters.description:
Exact desired output spatial shape.  Mutually exclusive with
`output_ratio`.

parameters.name: output_ratio
parameters.description:
Multiplicative shrinkage per axis.  Mutually exclusive with
`output_size`.

parameters.name: return_indices
parameters.description:
When `True`, also return per-output-cell argmax positions
(flattened `D × H × W` index).

parameters.name: _random_samples
parameters.description:
Per-instance uniform samples of shape `(N, C, 3)`.  Defaults
to fresh `lucid.rand`.

returns.description:
Output of shape `(N, C, oD, oH, oW)`; when
`return_indices`, also the argmax indices.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import fractional_max_pool3d
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 2, 8, 8, 8)
>>> y = fractional_max_pool3d(x, kernel_size=2, output_ratio=0.5)
>>> y.shape
(1, 2, 4, 4, 4)
notes:
Each axis runs Graham's start-position formula independently
(see `fractional_max_pool2d` Notes).  Implementation is a
pure-Python composite; gradients flow through `Tensor.max`.

members.name: dropout
members.signature: dropout(x: Tensor, p: float = 0.5, training: bool = True, inplace: bool = False)
members.summary: Element-wise Bernoulli dropout — the canonical Srivastava 2014 form.
members.extended:
During training, each scalar in `x` is independently zeroed with
probability `p` and survivors are scaled by $1 / (1 - p)$
so the expected activation magnitude stays unchanged.  At inference
(`training=False`) the call is a no-op identity.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input activations of any shape.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description:
Drop probability in $[0, 1)$.  `p = 0` short-circuits
to identity.  Default `0.5`.

parameters.name: training
parameters.description:
When `False`, returns `x` unchanged — identity at
inference time.  Modules built on top (`nn.Dropout`) pass
`self.training` here.  Default `True`.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description:
Accepted for API parity but currently ignored — the engine's
`nn.dropout` always returns a fresh tensor.  Default `False`.

returns.description: Same shape and dtype as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import dropout
>>> x = lucid.ones(2, 4)
>>> y = dropout(x, p=0.5, training=True)
>>> # ~50 % of entries are 0; survivors are 2.0 (= 1 / (1 - 0.5))
notes:
For each element $x_i$, draw an independent
$m_i \sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(1 - p)$ and apply



$$
y_i = \frac{m_i}{1 - p}\, x_i.
$$

Backward uses the *same* saved mask, so gradients also propagate
only through surviving positions.  For correlated activations
(sequence / image channels) prefer `dropout1d` /
`dropout2d` / `dropout3d`, which drop whole feature
maps and give stronger regularisation.

**Compile path routing.**  When called inside a
`lucid.compile._tracing` context with `training=True` and
`p > 0`, this wrapper allocates a Philox state buffer and routes
through the sibling engine op `_C_engine.nn.dropout_stateful`.
The captured trace then carries the 2-input/2-output schema the
compile path needs to plumb MPSGraph's stateful Philox RNG —
giving genuinely-per-dispatch varying masks where the stateless
seed path produces dispatch-deterministic ones.  Eager-only
callers (no active tracer) keep using the standard `nn.dropout`.

.. [1] Srivastava et al., *Dropout: A Simple Way to Prevent Neural
   Networks from Overfitting*, JMLR 2014.

members.name: dropout1d
members.signature: dropout1d(x: Tensor, p: float = 0.5, training: bool = True)
members.summary: Channel-wise dropout for 1-D sequence / feature inputs.
members.extended:
Drops entire 1-D feature maps (channels) of an $(N, C, L)$
tensor with probability `p`, scaling the survivors by
$1/(1-p)$ to keep the activation magnitude unbiased.
Standard per-element `dropout` is statistically weak when
applied within strongly-correlated feature maps (consecutive
positions in a sequence are highly correlated), so masking the
*whole* channel at once provides stronger regularisation in
1-D CNNs and temporal feature extractors.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor, typically of shape $(N, C, L)$.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Channel-drop probability in $[0, 1)$ (default `0.5`).

parameters.name: training
parameters.description:
When `False`, returns `x` unchanged — identity at
inference time (default `True`).

returns.description: Same shape and dtype as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import dropout1d
>>> x = lucid.ones((2, 4, 5))
>>> y = dropout1d(x, p=0.5, training=True)
notes:
For each batch element $n$ and channel $c$, draw
$m_{n,c} \sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(1 - p)$ independently and
apply



$$
y_{n, c, \ell} = \frac{m_{n, c}}{1 - p} \cdot x_{n, c, \ell}.
$$

The $1/(1-p)$ scale ("inverted dropout") preserves
$\mathbb{E}[y] = \mathbb{E}[x]$ so no rescaling is needed
at inference.

members.name: dropout2d
members.signature: dropout2d(x: Tensor, p: float = 0.5, training: bool = True)
members.summary: Channel-wise dropout for 2-D (spatial) feature maps.
members.extended:
Drops entire 2-D feature maps of an $(N, C, H, W)$ tensor
with probability `p` and rescales the survivors by
$1/(1-p)$.  This is the dropout variant of choice for
convolutional networks (Tompson et al. 2015 "SpatialDropout"):
adjacent pixels within a feature map are spatially correlated,
so elementwise dropout removes very little information.
Channel-wise masking forces independence between feature maps
instead.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of shape $(N, C, H, W)$.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Channel-drop probability in $[0, 1)$ (default `0.5`).

parameters.name: training
parameters.description: When `False`, identity (default `True`).

returns.description: Same shape and dtype as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import dropout2d
>>> x = lucid.ones((2, 8, 4, 4))
>>> y = dropout2d(x, p=0.5, training=True)
notes:
For each batch element $n$ and channel $c$, draw
$m_{n,c} \sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(1 - p)$ and broadcast
over the spatial dimensions:



$$
y_{n, c, h, w} = \frac{m_{n, c}}{1 - p} \cdot x_{n, c, h, w}.
$$

members.name: dropout3d
members.signature: dropout3d(x: Tensor, p: float = 0.5, training: bool = True)
members.summary: Channel-wise dropout for 3-D (volumetric) feature maps.
members.extended:
Drops entire 3-D feature maps of an $(N, C, D, H, W)$
tensor with probability `p` and rescales survivors by
$1/(1-p)$.  Used in 3-D ConvNets for video and volumetric
medical imaging, where spatio-temporal locality makes per-
element dropout statistically inefficient (same rationale as
`dropout2d`).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of shape $(N, C, D, H, W)$.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Channel-drop probability in $[0, 1)$ (default `0.5`).

parameters.name: training
parameters.description: When `False`, identity (default `True`).

returns.description: Same shape and dtype as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import dropout3d
>>> x = lucid.ones((1, 4, 3, 4, 4))
>>> y = dropout3d(x, p=0.5, training=True)
notes:
For each batch element $n$ and channel $c$, draw
$m_{n,c} \sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(1 - p)$ and broadcast
over the volumetric dimensions:



$$
y_{n, c, d, h, w} = \frac{m_{n, c}}{1 - p} \cdot x_{n, c, d, h, w}.
$$

members.name: alpha_dropout
members.signature: alpha_dropout(x: Tensor, p: float = 0.5, training: bool = True, inplace: bool = False)
members.summary: Alpha dropout — variance-preserving dropout for SELU networks.
members.extended:
Designed to be paired with the SELU activation (Klambauer
et al. 2017 "Self-Normalizing Neural Networks").  Standard
dropout breaks the carefully-tuned zero-mean / unit-variance
propagation that makes SELU networks self-normalising; alpha
dropout fixes this by replacing dropped activations with the
SELU saturation value $\alpha' = -\alpha \cdot \text{scale}$
(rather than zero), then applying an affine rescaling chosen so
that the post-dropout activations again have zero mean and unit
variance.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor, any shape.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Element-drop probability in $[0, 1]$ (default `0.5`).

parameters.name: training
parameters.description: When `False`, identity (default `True`).

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description:
Reserved for API parity; the implementation always
produces a new tensor (default `False`).

returns.description: Same shape and dtype as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import alpha_dropout
>>> x = lucid.randn(8)
>>> y = alpha_dropout(x, p=0.1, training=True)
notes:
With keep probability $q = 1 - p$, each element is
independently set to its original value (with probability
$q$) or to $\alpha'$ (with probability $p$).
The result is then affine-transformed:



$$
y = a \cdot \mathrm{mix}(x, \alpha') + b,
\quad
a = \big(q + p\,(\alpha')^2\,q\big)^{-1/2},
\quad
b = -a\,\alpha'\,p,
$$

chosen so that $\mathbb{E}[y] = 0$ and
$\mathrm{Var}(y) = 1$ when the input has zero mean and
unit variance.

members.name: feature_alpha_dropout
members.signature: feature_alpha_dropout(x: Tensor, p: float = 0.5, training: bool = True, inplace: bool = False)
members.summary: Channel-wise alpha dropout for SELU convolutional networks.
members.extended:
Combines the channel-granularity of `dropout2d` /
`dropout3d` with the variance-preserving substitution of
`alpha_dropout`.  Whole channels are dropped (replaced
rather than zeroed) and the result is affine-rescaled to keep
the self-normalising property — appropriate for SELU-activated
CNNs where spatially-correlated activations would make per-
element alpha dropout ineffective.

For inputs of rank less than 2, falls back to per-element
`alpha_dropout`.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description:
Input tensor of shape $(N, C, *)$ (one or more
spatial dimensions).

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Channel-drop probability in $[0, 1]$ (default `0.5`).

parameters.name: training
parameters.description: When `False`, identity (default `True`).

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Reserved for API parity; ignored (default `False`).

returns.description: Same shape and dtype as `x`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import feature_alpha_dropout
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 4, 3, 3)
>>> y = feature_alpha_dropout(x, p=0.25, training=True)
notes:
A Bernoulli mask of shape $(N, C)$ is drawn and
broadcast across the spatial axes; surviving channels are
multiplied by 1 and dropped channels by 0.  (The full alpha-
rescaling that `alpha_dropout` applies element-wise is
handled at the channel level here.)  Variance preservation
holds in expectation when the input statistics already satisfy
the SELU fixed-point assumptions.

members.name: scaled_dot_product_attention
members.signature: scaled_dot_product_attention(query: Tensor, key: Tensor, value: Tensor, attn_mask: Tensor | None = None, dropout_p: float = 0.0, is_causal: bool = False, scale: float | None = None)
members.summary: Scaled dot-product attention — the core of every Transformer block.
members.extended:
Computes the attention-weighted aggregation of value vectors using
query-key dot products as similarity scores:

$$
\mathrm{Attention}(Q, K, V) =
    \mathrm{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right) V
$$

where $M$ is an optional additive mask (`-inf` to disallow
attention at a position, `0` to allow).  The $1/\sqrt{d_k}$
factor keeps the softmax in a usable temperature regime as the head
dimension $d_k$ grows — without it, large dot products saturate
the softmax into a near one-hot distribution and gradients vanish.

parameters.name: query
parameters.description:
Shape `(B, H, T, E)`.  `B` batch, `H` heads, `T` query
positions, `E` head dimension $d_k$.

parameters.name: key
parameters.description: Shape `(B, H, S, E)`.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Shape `(B, H, S, V)`.  `V` may differ from `E`.

parameters.name: attn_mask
parameters.description:
Additive mask broadcast-compatible with `(B, H, T, S)`.  Use
large negative values (or `-inf`) at positions to mask out.
Mutually exclusive with `is_causal`.

parameters.name: dropout_p
parameters.description:
Dropout probability applied to attention weights during training.
Default `0.0`.

parameters.name: is_causal
parameters.description:
If `True`, apply an upper-triangular causal mask so each query
position only attends to keys at the same or earlier positions
(autoregressive decoder self-attention).

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description: Override the default $1/\sqrt{d_k}$ scale factor.

returns.description: Attention output of shape `(B, H, T, V)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import scaled_dot_product_attention
>>> q = lucid.randn(2, 8, 16, 64)          # (B, H, T, E)
>>> k = lucid.randn(2, 8, 16, 64)
>>> v = lucid.randn(2, 8, 16, 64)
>>> out = scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, is_causal=True)
>>> out.shape
(2, 8, 16, 64)
notes:
Introduced in *Attention Is All You Need* (Vaswani et al., 2017).
The implementation uses the log-sum-exp form of softmax for numerical
stability under aggressive masking, and fuses the scale into the
score matrix prior to softmax.  Causal masking enables efficient
autoregressive decoding when combined with a key/value cache.

members.name: mse_loss
members.signature: mse_loss(x: Tensor, target: Tensor, reduction: str = 'mean')
members.summary: Mean-squared-error (L2) loss between input and target.
members.extended:
The workhorse loss for regression problems.  Penalises large
errors *quadratically*, which makes it sensitive to outliers but
yields a well-conditioned optimisation surface — the gradient is
linear in the residual, so SGD updates scale gracefully near the
optimum.  Compare with `l1_loss` (constant gradient,
robust to outliers) and `huber_loss` (a smooth blend of
the two).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Predicted values, any shape.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: Target values; must be broadcast-compatible with `x`.

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description: `"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.

returns.description:
Scalar loss for `"mean"` / `"sum"`, or a per-element
tensor with `x`'s shape for `"none"`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import mse_loss
>>> pred = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
>>> target = lucid.tensor([1.5, 2.5, 2.5])
>>> mse_loss(pred, target)
Tensor(0.25)
notes:
Per-element loss:



$$
L_i = (x_i - y_i)^2
$$

With reduction:



$$
L = \begin{cases}
    \tfrac{1}{N}\sum_i L_i & \text{"mean"} \\
    \sum_i L_i & \text{"sum"} \\
    L_i & \text{"none"}
\end{cases}
$$

The gradient w.r.t. `x` is $2(x_i - y_i) / N$ under
`"mean"` reduction — proportional to the error, so updates
naturally shrink near the optimum.

members.name: l1_loss
members.signature: l1_loss(x: Tensor, target: Tensor, reduction: str = 'mean')
members.summary: Mean-absolute-error (L1) loss between input and target.
members.extended:
Robust regression loss whose gradient is constant in magnitude
($\pm 1$) and therefore *insensitive to outliers* —
extreme residuals do not dominate the update direction as they
do under `mse_loss`.  The trade-off is a non-smooth
optimisation surface at $x = y$ (sub-gradient at zero),
which can produce slightly slower convergence for small errors.
Often paired with `smooth_l1_loss` to recover smoothness
while keeping outlier robustness.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Predicted values, any shape.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: Target values; broadcast-compatible with `x`.

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description: `"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.

returns.description:
Scalar loss for `"mean"` / `"sum"`, or a per-element
tensor with `x`'s shape for `"none"`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import l1_loss
>>> pred = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
>>> target = lucid.tensor([1.5, 2.5, 2.5])
>>> l1_loss(pred, target)
Tensor(0.5)
notes:
Per-element loss:



$$
L_i = |x_i - y_i|
$$

Gradient w.r.t. `x` is $\operatorname{sign}(x_i - y_i)$
(zero at the origin).  Used heavily in image-to-image regression
(super-resolution, denoising) where outlier-robustness matters.

members.name: smooth_l1_loss
members.signature: smooth_l1_loss(x: Tensor, target: Tensor, beta: float = 1.0, reduction: str = 'mean')
members.summary: Smooth L1 loss — a quadratic-near-zero, linear-far-from-zero hybrid.
members.extended:
Combines the best of `mse_loss` and `l1_loss`: the
quadratic region near the origin gives a smooth gradient and
fast convergence, while the linear tails make the loss robust
to outliers.  This is the standard regression head used in
Fast R-CNN-style object detection (bounding-box regression),
where outlier bounding boxes would otherwise dominate the
training signal.

The function is a thin wrapper around `huber_loss` with
`delta = beta` and an additional `1/beta` scaling inside the
quadratic region (so the loss has unit slope at the transition).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Predicted values, any shape.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: Target values; broadcast-compatible with `x`.

parameters.name: beta
parameters.description:
Transition point between quadratic and linear regions
(default `1.0`).  Smaller `beta` makes the loss behave
more like `l1_loss`; larger `beta` makes it behave
more like `mse_loss`.

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description: `"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.

returns.description: Scalar (`"mean"`/`"sum"`) or full-shape (`"none"`).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import smooth_l1_loss
>>> pred = lucid.tensor([0.0, 2.0])
>>> target = lucid.tensor([0.5, 5.0])
>>> smooth_l1_loss(pred, target, beta=1.0)
Tensor(1.3125)

members.name: huber_loss
members.signature: huber_loss(x: Tensor, target: Tensor, delta: float = 1.0, reduction: str = 'mean')
members.summary: Huber loss — robust regression with a tunable transition point.
members.extended:
Identical in shape to `smooth_l1_loss` but parameterised
by the *slope-clip* point $\delta$ rather than the
quadratic-region scale $\beta$.  Inside the
$|x-y| < \delta$ region the loss is quadratic; outside,
the gradient saturates at $\pm\delta$ so a single huge
residual cannot dominate the update.

Originally proposed by Peter Huber (1964) as the maximum-
likelihood estimator for a contaminated Gaussian model — i.e.,
"mostly Gaussian noise but with occasional outliers".  Use it
when you suspect a small fraction of your residuals come from
a heavy-tailed distribution.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Predicted values.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: Target values; broadcast-compatible with `x`.

parameters.name: delta
parameters.description:
Threshold at which the loss transitions from quadratic to
linear (default `1.0`).

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description: `"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.

returns.description: Scalar or full-shape, per `reduction`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import huber_loss
>>> pred = lucid.tensor([0.0, 5.0])
>>> target = lucid.tensor([0.5, 0.0])
>>> huber_loss(pred, target, delta=1.0)
Tensor(2.3125)

members.name: cross_entropy
members.signature: cross_entropy(x: Tensor, target: Tensor, weight: Tensor | None = None, ignore_index: int = -100, reduction: str = 'mean', label_smoothing: float = 0.0)
members.summary: Cross-entropy loss for multi-class classification.
members.extended:
The canonical training objective for categorical classifiers.
Combines `lucid.nn.functional.log_softmax` and
`nll_loss` into a single, numerically stable expression —
operating directly on raw logits avoids the catastrophic
cancellation that arises when softmax probabilities are taken
to `log()`.  Implements the full contract: per-class
`weight` rescaling, `ignore_index` masking, and
label-smoothing regularisation.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Raw logits of shape $(N, C)$ or $(N, C, d_1, \dots, d_k)$.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description:
Either integer class indices of shape $(N,)$ /
$(N, d_1, \dots, d_k)$ or per-class probabilities of
shape matching `x`.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description:
Per-class weight vector of shape $(C,)$ — useful for
class-imbalanced training.

parameters.name: ignore_index
parameters.description:
Class index whose samples are skipped entirely (default `-100`).
Common for masked / padded targets in sequence models.

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description:
`"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.  Under
`"mean"`, the divisor is the sum of effective sample weights
(after `weight` and `ignore_index`), not the raw element
count.

parameters.name: label_smoothing
parameters.description:
Interpolation factor $\alpha \in [0, 1)$ between hard
one-hot targets and a uniform distribution
(Szegedy et al. 2016).  Acts as a regulariser by discouraging
over-confident predictions.

returns.description: Scalar (`"mean"`/`"sum"`) or per-sample tensor (`"none"`).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import cross_entropy
>>> logits = lucid.tensor([[2.0, 0.5, 0.1], [0.0, 1.5, 0.2]])
>>> target = lucid.tensor([0, 1])
>>> cross_entropy(logits, target)
Tensor(0.3490...)
notes:
Per-sample loss:



$$
L_i = -\sum_c w_c \, y_{i,c} \, \log \mathrm{softmax}(x_i)_c
$$

where $y_{i,c}$ is the (smoothed) target distribution.
With `label_smoothing = \alpha`,



$$
y_{i,c} = (1-\alpha)\,\mathbb{1}[c = t_i] + \alpha / C.
$$

Gradient w.r.t. the logits has the well-known clean form
$\mathrm{softmax}(x_i) - y_i$ (up to weighting), which is
the reason cross-entropy is preferred over MSE for classification:
no sigmoid saturation, no vanishing gradient.

members.name: nll_loss
members.signature: nll_loss(x: Tensor, target: Tensor, weight: Tensor | None = None, ignore_index: int = -100, reduction: str = 'mean')
members.summary: Negative log-likelihood loss for multi-class classification.
members.extended:
The "back-half" of `cross_entropy`: assumes the input is
already a tensor of log-probabilities (typically produced by
`lucid.nn.functional.log_softmax`).  Provided as a
separate entry-point so models that need log-probabilities for
downstream use (e.g., beam search) can avoid recomputing them.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description:
Log-probabilities of shape $(N, C)$ or
$(N, C, d_1, \dots, d_k)$.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description:
Integer class indices of shape $(N,)$ /
$(N, d_1, \dots, d_k)$.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description: Per-class weight vector $(C,)$.

parameters.name: ignore_index
parameters.description: Class index whose samples are excluded (default `-100`).

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description: `"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.

returns.description: Scalar or per-sample tensor depending on `reduction`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import nll_loss, log_softmax
>>> logits = lucid.tensor([[2.0, 0.5, 0.1], [0.0, 1.5, 0.2]])
>>> target = lucid.tensor([0, 1])
>>> nll_loss(log_softmax(logits, dim=1), target)
Tensor(0.3490...)
notes:
Per-sample loss:



$$
L_i = -w_{t_i}\,x_{i, t_i}
$$

Under `"mean"` reduction, the divisor is the sum of effective
sample weights — i.e., $\sum_i w_{t_i}\,\mathbb{1}[t_i \ne \text{ignore}]$
— not the raw count.  This matches the standard convention so
that the loss is invariant to a global rescaling of `weight`.

members.name: binary_cross_entropy
members.signature: binary_cross_entropy(x: Tensor, target: Tensor, weight: Tensor | None = None, reduction: str = 'mean')
members.summary: Binary cross-entropy between predicted probabilities and targets.
members.extended:
The standard objective for binary classification and for
multi-label classification with independent class predictions.
Operates on *probabilities* in $(0, 1)$ — typically the
output of a `lucid.nn.functional.sigmoid`.  When working
with raw logits, prefer `binary_cross_entropy_with_logits`
for numerical stability.

Inputs are clamped to $[\varepsilon, 1 - \varepsilon]$
with $\varepsilon = 10^{-12}$ before the logarithm to
prevent `-inf` gradients at the boundaries.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Predicted probabilities in $(0, 1)$, any shape.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: Target probabilities (typically binary) of the same shape.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description:
Element-wise rescaling factor (broadcast-compatible with
`x`).  Use to up-weight rare classes or hard examples.

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description: `"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.

returns.description: Scalar or full-shape per `reduction`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import binary_cross_entropy
>>> p = lucid.tensor([0.9, 0.2, 0.7])
>>> y = lucid.tensor([1.0, 0.0, 1.0])
>>> binary_cross_entropy(p, y)
Tensor(0.2284...)
notes:
Per-element loss:



$$
L_i = -\big(y_i\,\log p_i + (1 - y_i)\,\log(1 - p_i)\big)
$$

Gradient w.r.t. `x` is $(p_i - y_i) / (p_i(1 - p_i))$,
which diverges as $p_i \to 0$ or $1$ — the reason
the logits-form (which yields a clean $\sigma(x) - y$
gradient) is preferred when training stability is critical.

members.name: binary_cross_entropy_with_logits
members.signature: binary_cross_entropy_with_logits(x: Tensor, target: Tensor, weight: Tensor | None = None, pos_weight: Tensor | None = None, reduction: str = 'mean')
members.summary: Binary cross-entropy from raw logits (numerically stable).
members.extended:
Mathematically equivalent to
`binary_cross_entropy(sigmoid(x), target)` but evaluated in a
log-sum-exp-style form that avoids overflow/underflow when
`|x|` is large.  This is the preferred binary classification
loss for training — composing a separate sigmoid with BCE risks
catastrophic cancellation in $\log(1 - \sigma(x))$ for
large positive logits.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Raw logits (un-bounded reals), any shape.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: Target probabilities (typically binary), same shape as `x`.

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description: Element-wise rescaling factor.

parameters.name: pos_weight
parameters.description:
Per-class weight applied to the *positive* term only —
useful for highly-imbalanced binary tasks, where setting
`pos_weight = n_neg / n_pos` recovers the prevalence-
balanced gradient.

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description: `"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.

returns.description: Scalar or full-shape per `reduction`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import binary_cross_entropy_with_logits
>>> logits = lucid.tensor([2.0, -1.0, 0.5])
>>> target = lucid.tensor([1.0, 0.0, 1.0])
>>> binary_cross_entropy_with_logits(logits, target)
Tensor(0.3567...)
notes:
The numerically stable base form is



$$
L_i = \max(x_i, 0) - x_i\,y_i + \log\!\big(1 + e^{-|x_i|}\big),
$$

equivalent to $-(y\log\sigma(x) + (1-y)\log(1-\sigma(x)))$
but free of overflow.  With `pos_weight`:



$$
L_i = (1 - y_i)\,x_i + \big(1 + (w^{+} - 1) y_i\big)
      \big(\log(1 + e^{-|x_i|}) + \max(-x_i, 0)\big).
$$

Gradient w.r.t. `x` is the clean $\sigma(x_i) - y_i$
(modulo weighting) — the canonical reason this form is used in
practice instead of the explicit sigmoid + BCE composition.

members.name: kl_div
members.signature: kl_div(x: Tensor, target: Tensor, size_average: bool | None = None, reduction: str = 'mean', log_target: bool = False)
members.summary: Kullback-Leibler divergence between two distributions.
members.extended:
Measures the "information gain" from approximating distribution
$p$ (the target) with distribution $q$ (the input).
Used heavily in knowledge distillation (matching student logits
to a teacher's), variational inference (the ELBO's KL term),
and policy-gradient regularisation in RL.

The convention here matches the reference framework: `x` is
$\log q$ (log-predicted), and `target` is $p$
(target probability) — or $\log p$ if `log_target=True`.
Note this is asymmetric in its arguments — KL is *not* a metric.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description:
Log-probabilities of the *predicted* distribution
$\log q$, any shape.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description:
Probabilities of the *target* distribution $p$,
or its log when `log_target=True`.  Same shape as `x`.

parameters.name: size_average
parameters.description:
Deprecated.  Retained for signature compatibility; ignored
— use `reduction` instead.

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description:
`"none"`, `"mean"`, `"sum"`, or `"batchmean"`
(default `"mean"`).  `"batchmean"` divides the summed
loss by the leading (batch) dimension and is the *only*
reduction that yields the mathematically correct KL value
in expectation.

parameters.name: log_target
parameters.description:
When `True`, treat `target` as already-logged
($\log p$).  This often avoids a redundant
$\log$ / $\exp$ round-trip.

returns.description: Scalar or full-shape per `reduction`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import kl_div, log_softmax
>>> log_q = log_softmax(lucid.tensor([[2.0, 0.5, 0.1]]), dim=1)
>>> p = lucid.tensor([[0.8, 0.15, 0.05]])
>>> kl_div(log_q, p, reduction="batchmean")
Tensor(0.0641...)
notes:
Per-element loss:



$$
L_i = p_i \cdot (\log p_i - \log q_i)
$$

Globally:



$$
D_{\mathrm{KL}}(p \,\|\, q) = \sum_i p_i \log \tfrac{p_i}{q_i} \ge 0,
$$

with equality iff $p = q$ almost everywhere.  The standard
`"mean"` reduction divides by the element count, not the
batch size, so it under-reports the divergence value — prefer
`"batchmean"` whenever the absolute scale matters.

members.name: triplet_margin_loss
members.signature: triplet_margin_loss(anchor: Tensor, positive: Tensor, negative: Tensor, margin: float = 1.0, p: float = 2.0, eps: float = 1e-06, swap: bool = False, reduction: str = 'mean')
members.summary: Triplet margin loss for metric learning.
members.extended:
Pulls an "anchor" embedding toward a "positive" example (same
class / matching pair) and pushes it away from a "negative"
example by at least a fixed `margin`.  This is the workhorse
objective for learning embeddings used in face verification
(FaceNet), image retrieval, and contrastive representation
learning.

Optionally applies the "anchor-swap" trick of Balntas et al.
2016 — when `d(p, n) < d(a, n)` the positive is closer to the
negative than the anchor is, so we swap roles and use the
*harder* of the two distances, focusing gradient on the more
informative triplet.

parameters.name: anchor
parameters.description: Embedding of shape $(N, D)$.

parameters.name: positive
parameters.description: Positive sample embedding of the same shape.

parameters.name: negative
parameters.description: Negative sample embedding of the same shape.

parameters.name: margin
parameters.description:
Minimum desired gap between positive and negative distances
(default `1.0`).  Triplets satisfying the margin already
receive zero loss / zero gradient.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description:
Norm degree of the pairwise distance (default `2.0` —
Euclidean).

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description:
Numerical floor inside the distance to avoid zero-derivative
at coincident points (default `1e-6`).

parameters.name: swap
parameters.description: Enable the Balntas-anchor-swap trick (default `False`).

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description: `"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.

returns.description: Scalar or per-triplet tensor of shape $(N,)$.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import triplet_margin_loss
>>> a = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 0.0]])
>>> p = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 0.1]])
>>> n = lucid.tensor([[0.0, 1.0]])
>>> triplet_margin_loss(a, p, n, margin=1.0)
Tensor(0.6862...)
notes:
Per-triplet loss:



$$
L_i = \max\!\big(0,\; d(a_i, p_i) - d(a_i, n_i) + \text{margin}\big),
$$

where $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ is the $L_p$ pairwise
distance.  Easy triplets (already separated by `margin`)
contribute exactly zero — only "semi-hard" / "hard" triplets
drive the update, which is why batch mining strategies matter
so much in practice.

members.name: triplet_margin_with_distance_loss
members.signature: triplet_margin_with_distance_loss(anchor: Tensor, positive: Tensor, negative: Tensor, distance_function: object | None = None, margin: float = 1.0, swap: bool = False, reduction: str = 'mean')
members.summary: Triplet margin loss with a user-supplied distance function.
members.extended:
Identical in form to `triplet_margin_loss` but lets the
caller plug in any binary distance callable — useful when the
embedding space is non-Euclidean (e.g., learned Mahalanobis
distances, cosine distance, or hyperbolic embeddings).  When
no `distance_function` is supplied it defaults to the
$L_2$ pairwise distance, matching the reference framework
semantics.

parameters.name: anchor
parameters.description: Anchor embedding of shape $(N, D)$.

parameters.name: positive
parameters.description: Positive sample embedding of the same shape.

parameters.name: negative
parameters.description: Negative sample embedding of the same shape.

parameters.name: distance_function
parameters.description:
Function `(x, y) -> Tensor` returning a non-negative
distance of shape $(N,)$.  Defaults to $L_2$
pairwise distance.

parameters.name: margin
parameters.description:
Minimum desired margin between positive and negative
distances (default `1.0`).

parameters.name: swap
parameters.description:
Enable the Balntas-2016 anchor-swap heuristic: replace
$d(a, n)$ with $\min\!\big(d(a, n), d(p, n)\big)$
so the harder negative drives the gradient (default `False`).

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description: `"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.

returns.description: Scalar or per-triplet tensor.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import (
...     triplet_margin_with_distance_loss,
...     pairwise_distance,
... )
>>> def manhattan(a, b):
...     return pairwise_distance(a, b, p=1.0)
>>> a = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 0.0]])
>>> p = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 0.1]])
>>> n = lucid.tensor([[0.0, 1.0]])
>>> triplet_margin_with_distance_loss(a, p, n, distance_function=manhattan)
Tensor(0.2)
notes:
Per-triplet loss:



$$
L_i = \max\!\big(0,\; d(a_i, p_i) - d(a_i, n_i) + \text{margin}\big)
$$

The Lucid module wrapper
`lucid.nn.TripletMarginWithDistanceLoss` forwards into
this function; both surfaces are valid entry-points.

members.name: cosine_embedding_loss
members.signature: cosine_embedding_loss(x1: Tensor, x2: Tensor, y: Tensor, margin: float = 0.0, reduction: str = 'mean')
members.summary: Cosine embedding loss for pairwise similarity learning.
members.extended:
Encourages "similar" pairs (label $y = 1$) to align in
direction and "dissimilar" pairs ($y = -1$) to be at
least `margin` cosine-units apart.  Operates purely on
angular (direction) information — magnitudes are normalised
away, which is useful when the relevant signal is the
*direction* of embeddings (e.g., word vectors, learned
representations).

parameters.name: x1
parameters.description: First embedding of shape $(N, D)$.

parameters.name: x2
parameters.description: Second embedding of the same shape.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: Label tensor of shape $(N,)$ with values $\pm 1$.

parameters.name: margin
parameters.description:
Minimum desired cosine gap for dissimilar pairs, typically
in $[-1, 1]$ (default `0.0`).

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description: `"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.

returns.description: Scalar or per-pair tensor of shape $(N,)$.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import cosine_embedding_loss
>>> x1 = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 0.0]])
>>> x2 = lucid.tensor([[0.5, 0.5]])
>>> y = lucid.tensor([1.0])
>>> cosine_embedding_loss(x1, x2, y)
Tensor(0.2928...)
notes:
Per-pair loss:



$$
L_i = \begin{cases}
    1 - \cos(x_1^{(i)}, x_2^{(i)}) & y_i = +1 \\
    \max\!\big(0,\; \cos(x_1^{(i)}, x_2^{(i)}) - \text{margin}\big) & y_i = -1
\end{cases}
$$

With `margin = 0`, dissimilar pairs are only penalised when
they have positive cosine similarity — i.e., the loss is
satisfied as long as the angle between them exceeds $90°$.
Increasing `margin` toward 1 demands more separation.

members.name: margin_ranking_loss
members.signature: margin_ranking_loss(x1: Tensor, x2: Tensor, y: Tensor, margin: float = 0.0, reduction: str = 'mean')
members.summary: Pairwise ranking hinge loss.
members.extended:
Trains a scoring function so that for each pair $(x_1, x_2)$
the *signed* score gap $y\,(x_1 - x_2)$ exceeds the
`margin`.  Used for learning-to-rank (search, recommendation),
Bradley-Terry style preference modelling, and reward-model
training for RLHF — wherever the supervision signal is a
pairwise preference rather than a target value.

parameters.name: x1
parameters.description: Scores for the first item of each pair, any shape.

parameters.name: x2
parameters.description: Scores for the second item, same shape as `x1`.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description:
Pairwise preference label $\pm 1$: $+1$ if
$x_1$ should rank higher, $-1$ otherwise.

parameters.name: margin
parameters.description: Required minimum score gap (default `0.0`).

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description: `"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.

returns.description: Scalar or per-pair tensor.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import margin_ranking_loss
>>> s1 = lucid.tensor([2.0, 0.5])
>>> s2 = lucid.tensor([1.0, 1.0])
>>> y = lucid.tensor([1.0, 1.0])
>>> margin_ranking_loss(s1, s2, y, margin=1.0)
Tensor(0.75)
notes:
Per-pair loss:



$$
L_i = \max\!\big(0,\; -y_i\,(x_1^{(i)} - x_2^{(i)}) + \text{margin}\big)
$$

Pairs already satisfying the margin ($y(x_1 - x_2) \ge \text{margin}$)
contribute zero loss and zero gradient — the hinge structure
naturally focuses learning on the violating pairs, akin to a
pairwise SVM.

members.name: hinge_embedding_loss
members.signature: hinge_embedding_loss(x: Tensor, y: Tensor, margin: float = 1.0, reduction: str = 'mean')
members.summary: Hinge embedding loss.
members.extended:
Designed for similarity learning on pre-computed distances:
given a (typically non-negative) score `x` representing a
pairwise distance, push positive pairs (label $+1$)
toward small distances and negative pairs (label $-1$)
above a fixed `margin`.  Common in Siamese network training
and energy-based dissimilarity models.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Per-pair score (distance) tensor, any shape.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: Label tensor $\pm 1$ with the same shape as `x`.

parameters.name: margin
parameters.description: Margin enforced for negative pairs (default `1.0`).

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description: `"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.

returns.description: Scalar or full-shape per `reduction`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import hinge_embedding_loss
>>> dist = lucid.tensor([0.2, 0.8])
>>> y = lucid.tensor([1.0, -1.0])
>>> hinge_embedding_loss(dist, y, margin=1.0)
Tensor(0.2)
notes:
Per-element loss:



$$
L_i = \begin{cases}
    x_i & y_i = +1 \\
    \max(0,\; \text{margin} - x_i) & y_i = -1
\end{cases}
$$

The positive branch simply minimises the distance; the negative
branch is a one-sided hinge that pushes apart only those pairs
whose distance is below the margin — pairs already far apart
contribute nothing.  This asymmetric structure prevents the
loss from collapsing all embeddings into a single point.

members.name: poisson_nll_loss
members.signature: poisson_nll_loss(x: Tensor, target: Tensor, log_input: bool = True, full: bool = False, eps: float = 1e-08, reduction: str = 'mean')
members.summary: Poisson negative log-likelihood loss for count regression.
members.extended:
The maximum-likelihood objective when targets are non-negative
integer counts modelled as $y \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda)$.
Standard for forecasting tasks (web clicks, event counts,
biological cell counts) where the variance scales with the
mean.  Unlike `mse_loss`, this loss respects the
heteroscedasticity inherent in count data.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description:
Predicted Poisson rate.  By default (`log_input=True`)
treated as $\log \lambda$ for numerical stability;
set `log_input=False` to pass the rate $\lambda$
directly.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: Observed counts, broadcast-compatible with `x`.

parameters.name: log_input
parameters.description:
Whether `x` is $\log \lambda$ (default) or
$\lambda$.  The log-form avoids exponentiating an
unbounded prediction in inner loops.

parameters.name: full
parameters.description:
Include the Stirling approximation term
$\log(y!) \approx y\log y - y + \tfrac{1}{2}\log(2\pi y)$
in the loss.  Has no effect on gradients (constant in
`x`) but yields the correct log-likelihood value.  Not
currently added — kept for API parity.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description:
Small constant added before $\log$ when
`log_input=False` (default `1e-8`).

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description: `"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.

returns.description: Scalar or full-shape per `reduction`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import poisson_nll_loss
>>> log_lam = lucid.tensor([0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> y = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 5.0])
>>> poisson_nll_loss(log_lam, y, log_input=True)
Tensor(0.7299...)

members.name: gaussian_nll_loss
members.signature: gaussian_nll_loss(x: Tensor, target: Tensor, var: Tensor, full: bool = False, eps: float = 1e-06, reduction: str = 'mean')
members.summary: Gaussian negative log-likelihood for heteroscedastic regression.
members.extended:
Maximum-likelihood objective when the prediction is a
*distribution* $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ over the
target rather than a point estimate.  Training a network with
two heads (one for $\mu$, one for $\sigma^2$)
against this loss recovers calibrated predictive uncertainty
— useful for active learning, decision-aware regression, and
Bayesian deep ensembles.

The variance `var` is clamped below by `eps` to prevent
division by zero and runaway log-terms when the network
initially predicts near-zero variance.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Predicted means $\mu$, any shape.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: Observed values $y$, broadcast-compatible with `x`.

parameters.name: var
parameters.description:
Predicted variances $\sigma^2 > 0$, broadcast-
compatible with `x`.

parameters.name: full
parameters.description:
Include the constant $\tfrac{1}{2}\log(2\pi)$ term in
the loss value.  Has no effect on gradients; useful only
for reporting log-likelihoods.  Not currently added.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description:
Lower bound applied to `var` for numerical stability
(default `1e-6`).

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description: `"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.

returns.description: Scalar or full-shape per `reduction`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import gaussian_nll_loss
>>> mu = lucid.tensor([0.0, 1.0])
>>> y = lucid.tensor([0.5, 1.0])
>>> var = lucid.tensor([1.0, 0.25])
>>> gaussian_nll_loss(mu, y, var)
Tensor(-0.2218...)
notes:
Per-element loss (constant terms dropped):



$$
L_i = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\log \sigma_i^2 + \frac{(y_i - \mu_i)^2}{\sigma_i^2}\right)
$$

The first term penalises over-confidence (small variance), the
second term rewards accuracy weighted by precision.  Together
they give the model a clean trade-off: when it cannot reduce
$(y-\mu)^2$, increasing $\sigma^2$ decreases the
loss — this is what produces calibrated uncertainty estimates.

members.name: ctc_loss
members.signature: ctc_loss(log_probs: Tensor, targets: Tensor, input_lengths: Tensor, target_lengths: Tensor, blank: int = 0, reduction: str = 'mean', zero_infinity: bool = False)
members.summary: Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) loss.
members.extended:
The standard training objective for unaligned sequence
prediction — used in speech recognition, handwriting
recognition, and any task where the input sequence is much
longer than the target and no per-frame alignment is provided.
Introduced by Graves et al. 2006.

Internally marginalises over every valid alignment of a
$T$-frame prediction onto an $S$-symbol target by
inserting "blank" symbols and allowing each target symbol to
span one or more frames, computing the negative log of the
total path probability via dynamic programming.

parameters.name: log_probs
parameters.description:
Log-probabilities of shape $(T, N, C)$ where
$T$ is the input sequence length, $N$ is the
batch size, and $C$ is the number of classes
(including the blank).  Typically produced by
`lucid.nn.functional.log_softmax` over the class
axis.

parameters.name: targets
parameters.description:
Target indices, shape $(N, S)$ (padded) or
$(\sum_i \text{target\_lengths}_i,)$ (concatenated).
`int32`.

parameters.name: input_lengths
parameters.description:
Effective input lengths $(N,)$, `int32`.  Enables
padding-aware batching.

parameters.name: target_lengths
parameters.description: Effective target lengths $(N,)$, `int32`.

parameters.name: blank
parameters.description: Index of the blank symbol (default `0`).

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description:
`"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.  Under
`"mean"`, the per-sample loss is averaged across the
batch.

parameters.name: zero_infinity
parameters.description:
When `True`, infinite losses (which arise when a target
cannot fit in the available input frames) and their
gradients are set to zero, effectively skipping those
samples (default `False`).

returns.description:
Scalar (`"mean"` / `"sum"`) or per-sample tensor of
shape $(N,)$.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import ctc_loss, log_softmax
>>> # T=4 frames, N=1 batch, C=3 classes (blank=0)
>>> logits = lucid.randn(4, 1, 3)
>>> log_p = log_softmax(logits, dim=2)
>>> targets = lucid.tensor([[1, 2]], dtype=lucid.int32)
>>> il = lucid.tensor([4], dtype=lucid.int32)
>>> tl = lucid.tensor([2], dtype=lucid.int32)
>>> ctc_loss(log_p, targets, il, tl)
Tensor(...)
notes:
The CTC objective is the negative log of the total alignment
probability:



$$
L = -\log \sum_{\pi \in \mathcal{B}^{-1}(\mathbf{y})}
    \prod_{t=1}^{T} p_t(\pi_t),
$$

where $\mathcal{B}$ is the "many-to-one" alignment map
that collapses repeats and removes blanks.  The forward DP runs
in log-domain (Accelerate arithmetic on CPU); the GPU stream
currently falls back to CPU.

members.name: multi_margin_loss
members.signature: multi_margin_loss(x: Tensor, target: Tensor, p: int = 1, margin: float = 1.0, weight: Tensor | None = None, reduction: str = 'mean')
members.summary: Multi-class hinge (margin) loss — Crammer-Singer SVM objective.
members.extended:
A non-probabilistic alternative to `cross_entropy` for
multi-class classification: instead of fitting a softmax
distribution, it requires the true-class score to exceed every
other class score by at least `margin`.  Frequently used in
structured prediction and as a drop-in for hinge-style losses
in metric learning.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Class scores of shape $(N, C)$.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: Integer class indices of shape $(N,)$.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description:
Power applied to each hinge term — `1` for the standard
hinge loss, `2` for the smoother squared-hinge variant
(default `1`).

parameters.name: margin
parameters.description:
Required minimum score gap between the true class and
every competitor (default `1.0`).

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description:
Per-class weight vector of shape $(C,)$.  Each sample
contribution is scaled by the weight of its *true* class.

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description: `"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.

returns.description: Scalar or per-sample tensor of shape $(N,)$.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import multi_margin_loss
>>> scores = lucid.tensor([[2.0, 0.5, 0.1]])
>>> target = lucid.tensor([0], dtype=lucid.int32)
>>> multi_margin_loss(scores, target)
Tensor(0.0)
notes:
Per-sample loss:



$$
L_i = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{j \ne t_i}
      \max\!\big(0,\; \text{margin} - x_{i, t_i} + x_{i, j}\big)^p
$$

Samples whose true-class score already dominates all
competitors by `margin` produce zero loss and zero gradient
— like the binary SVM hinge, only the *support vectors*
contribute to the update.

members.name: multilabel_margin_loss
members.signature: multilabel_margin_loss(x: Tensor, target: Tensor, reduction: str = 'mean')
members.summary: Multi-label hinge loss for set-valued targets.
members.extended:
The multi-label counterpart of `multi_margin_loss`: each
sample can belong to *several* classes (the "positives"), and
the objective requires every positive class score to exceed
every non-positive (negative) class score by at least 1.  Used
for set prediction tasks such as image tagging where labels
are not mutually exclusive.

Targets are encoded as a fixed-width index list with `-1` as
a padding sentinel — entries up to the first `-1` mark the
positive classes for that sample.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Class scores of shape $(N, C)$ or $(C,)$.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description:
Same shape as `x`.  Non-negative entries are positive
class indices; `-1` entries are ignored.

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description: `"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.

returns.description: Scalar or per-sample tensor of shape $(N,)$.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import multilabel_margin_loss
>>> scores = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.2]])
>>> target = lucid.tensor([[0, 1, -1, -1]], dtype=lucid.int32)
>>> multilabel_margin_loss(scores, target)
Tensor(0.85)
notes:
Per-sample loss, summed over positive labels $t$ and
non-positive labels $j$:



$$
L_i = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{t \in P_i} \sum_{j \notin P_i}
      \max\!\big(0,\; 1 - x_{i, t} + x_{i, j}\big),
$$

where $P_i$ is the set of positive labels for sample
$i$.  Equivalently, it is the average of multi-class
hinge losses obtained by treating each positive label as
*the* correct one against the full set of non-positives.

members.name: soft_margin_loss
members.signature: soft_margin_loss(input: Tensor, target: Tensor, reduction: str = 'mean')
members.summary: Logistic (softplus) loss for binary classification with ±1 labels.
members.extended:
A "soft" variant of the binary hinge loss: instead of the
piecewise-linear $\max(0, 1 - y\,x)$, it uses the smooth
surrogate $\log(1 + e^{-y\,x})$ — which is the
negative-log-likelihood of a logistic model with labels in
$\{-1, +1\}$.  Equivalent to
`binary_cross_entropy_with_logits` with the `{0, 1}`
labels re-coded as $\{-1, +1\}$.

The implementation evaluates $\mathrm{softplus}(-y\,x)$,
which is numerically stable for large `|x|` (no overflow,
no log of near-zero values).

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Raw scores (logits), any shape.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description:
Target tensor of the same shape, conventionally holding
$\pm 1$ (any real values are accepted).

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description: `"mean"` (default), `"sum"`, or `"none"`.

returns.description: Scalar or full-shape per `reduction`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.functional import soft_margin_loss
>>> x = lucid.tensor([2.0, -1.0])
>>> y = lucid.tensor([1.0, -1.0])
>>> soft_margin_loss(x, y)
Tensor(0.2284...)
notes:
Per-element loss:



$$
L_i = \log\!\big(1 + \exp(-y_i\,x_i)\big).
$$

Unlike the (non-smooth) hinge, every sample contributes a
